values.raw_excerpt: (b) "entity" means any consumer who has contracted demand or sanctioned load of 100 kW or more except for captive consumers: Provided that in case of captive consumers there shall not be any load limitation;
values.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 2(1)(b) [Definitions] and Rule 5(2) proviso (Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II Sec 3(i), notified 6 June 2022)
values.caveat: Threshold of 100 kW contracted demand or sanctioned load applies to all consumers except captive consumers (no limit). Aggregable across single or multiple connections in the same electricity division per 2023 amendments. Figure confirmed current as of 2023 and 2023 Second Amendment Rules.

values.text: CSS shall not be increased, during twelve years from the date of operating of the generating plant using renewable energy sources, by more than fifty percent of the surcharge fixed for the year in which open access is granted.
values.raw_excerpt: Provided that the cross subsidy surcharge for Green Energy Open Access Consumer purchasing green energy, from a generating plant using renewable energy sources, shall not be increased, during twelve years from the date of operating of the generating plant using renewable energy sources, by more than fifty percent of the surcharge fixed for the year in which open access is granted;
values.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 9(2) proviso (Charges to be levied for Open Access)
values.caveat: National framework does NOT fix a CSS concession percentage; it CAPS the ESCALATION at +50% of the granting-year value over the first 12 years of plant operation. Absolute CSS quantum is state-commission determined. CSS (and additional surcharge) fully waived for green hydrogen/ammonia and waste-to-energy supply; CSS exempt for captive consumers under the Electricity Act. Do not treat as a fixed discount percentage.

values.raw_excerpt: Provided further that the additional surcharge shall not be applicable for Green Energy Open Access Consumers, if fixed charges are being paid by such a consumer: Provided also that cross subsidy surcharge and additional surcharge shall not be applicable in case power produced from a Waste-to-Energy plant is supplied to the Open Access Consumer. Provided also that Cross subsidy surcharge and additional surcharge shall not be applicable if green energy is utilized for production of green hydrogen and green ammonia.
values.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 9(2) provisos
values.caveat: Additional surcharge is applicable in principle (state-set to cover DISCOM fixed/stranded costs) but the national rule WAIVES it conditionally: for GEOA consumers if fixed charges are paid, and unconditionally for waste-to-energy supply and green hydrogen/ammonia production. The surcharge quantum is state-determined; only the waiver conditions are national.

values.raw_excerpt: 8. Banking.– (1) Banking shall be permitted at least on a monthly basis on payment of charges to compensate additional costs, if any, to the distribution licensee by the Banking and the Appropriate Commission shall fix the applicable charges. (2) The permitted quantum of banked energy by the Green Energy Open Access consumers shall be at least thirty percent of the total monthly consumption of electricity from the distribution licensee by the consumers.
values.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 8 (Banking)
values.caveat: Banking mandatorily permitted at least monthly, for at least 30% of monthly DISCOM consumption. Charges and any quantum above 30% are fixed by the State/Appropriate Commission.

values.raw_excerpt: Provided that the credit for banked energy shall not be permitted to be carried forward to subsequent months and the credit of energy banked during the month shall be adjusted during the same month.
values.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 8(2) Explanation/proviso
values.caveat: Monthly banking is the national minimum settlement period; banked credit must be adjusted within the same month and cannot be carried forward. The 2022 Rules standardized this nationally; some states historically allowed annual/seasonal banking but monthly is the current national floor.

values.raw_excerpt: (B) By procuring Renewable Energy through Open Access from any Developer either directly or through a trading licensee or through power markets.
values.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 4(2)(B)
values.caveat: Third-party sale is allowed; any entity may procure renewable energy through open access directly from any developer, via a trading licensee, or through power markets. The open-access charges (wheeling/CSS/transmission) applicable to third-party sale are state-determined.

values.raw_excerpt: (A) Own Generation from renewable energy sources.–There shall not be any capacity limit for installation of power plants from renewable energy sources, by entities for their own consumption and such plants may be set up at any location in India and power shall be transmitted by using open access ... Provided that in case of captive consumers there shall not be any load limitation;
values.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 4(2)(A) and Rule 2(1)(b)
values.caveat: Captive/own-generation explicitly allowed with no capacity limit and no load limitation for captive consumers; plants may be located anywhere in India and wheeled via open access. CSS not payable for captive consumers under the Electricity Act. Captive-status compliance (26%/51% rules) governed separately by the Electricity Rules 2005.

values.raw_excerpt: Renewable energy power plants in the solar, wind and hydropower sectors have must-run status ... A must-run power plant will not be subjected to curtailment or regulation of generation or supply of electricity on account of merit order dispatch or any other commercial consideration except technical constraint in the electricity grid or for reasons of security of the electricity grid.
values.source_name: CERC (Indian Electricity Grid Code) Regulations, 2023 - must-run status for solar/wind (CERC Current Regulations page; reflected in CERC curtailment orders)
values.caveat: Must-run status for solar/wind under CERC IEGC Regulations 2023 (Regulation 49(1)(f)(iii)); LDCs are statutorily responsible for evacuating all available solar/wind generation. Curtailment permitted only for grid-security/technical constraints. Summarized from secondary legal sources and CERC orders; recommend pinning to exact IEGC 2023 clause for definitive gazette quote.

values.text: IREDA additional interest-rate rebate of 0.25% over the new-wind-project rate; PFC/REC/IREDA loans on same terms as new projects; all fiscal/financial benefits of new wind projects extended to repowering; micro-siting flexibility.
values.raw_excerpt: IREDA is required to provide an additional interest rate rebate of 0.25% over and above the interest rate available to new wind projects. ... REC/PFC/IREDA will provide loans for repowering projects on the same terms, and conditions as laid down for new projects. ... All fiscal and financial benefits available to new wind projects will be available to repowering projects as well.
values.source_name: MNRE National Repowering & Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects – 2023 (circular dated 7 Dec 2023, superseding the 5 Aug 2016 Repowering Policy) - quoted by Argus Partners regulatory update
values.caveat: Issued by MNRE on 7 Dec 2023, supersedes the 5 Aug 2016 policy. Eligibility: turbines <2 MW, design-life-completed turbines, non-QCO-compliant turbines, or voluntary after 15 years; identified repowering potential ~25.406 GW. Must be operational within 24 months of consent letter. Value extracted from law-firm summary; recommend linking to primary MNRE PDF on mnre.gov.in for authoritative gazette/circular text. Charges/banking benefits implemented via state commissions.

values.raw_excerpt: 6. Nodal Agency.– (1) A Central Nodal Agency shall be notified by the Central Government to set up and operate a single window green energy open access system for renewable energy.
values.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 6(1)
values.caveat: A Central Nodal Agency operates a single-window GEOA system via a centralised registry/portal (GOAR). Applications approved within 15 days (deemed approved otherwise). Single-window applies to GEOA registration/approval; project-level land/NOC clearances remain state/local responsibilities.

values.raw_excerpt: Commission decides that procurement of wind power by the Distribution Licensee, for compliance of RPO requirement, shall be through the competitive bidding route under section 63 of the Electricity Act 2003 ... The CERC has amended the RE Tariff regulations such that no annual generic tariff shall be determined in the case of Wind and Solar power.
values.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 - Order on Procurement of Wind Power and Related Issues
values.caveat: Order No. 8 of 2020 (effective 07.10.2020) governs new wind procurement via competitive bidding. For RE generators encashing surplus/banked energy where no bid tariff exists, the APPC-capped-at-75%-of-preferential mechanism (RPO Amendment 2024) applies.

values.raw_excerpt: consumers, traders, distribution licensees and RE generating companies shall be eligible for Green Energy Open Access ... Open Access shall be allowed to all EHT & HT consumers with minimum contracted demand of 63 kVA and above subject to the terms and conditions of supply.
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: Third-party open-access sale is permitted (subject to all OA charges). GEOA 2025 notified 18-09-2025, effective 01-10-2025. Note: no banking facility for third-party power purchase/sale (see banking_third_party).

values.raw_excerpt: Every person, who is qualified as a Captive Generating Plant (CGP) shall have the right to open access as per the provisions of Section 9 of the Act read with rules and Regulations framed under the Act
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: Captive generating plants have an automatic statutory right to open access under Section 9 of the Electricity Act, 2003. Effective 01-10-2025.

values.text: 63 kVA (contracted demand for EHT/HT consumers); equivalent to approximately 50 kW connected load at 0.8 power factor
values.raw_excerpt: Green Energy Open Access Customer means all EHT & HT consumers with minimum contracted demand of 63 kVA and above ... The Rule framed by the MoP has suggested to allow GEOA to all consumers who have contracted demand or sanctioned load of 100 kW and above ... Open Access is being allowed now to all the EHT/HT consumers with the contracted demand of 63 kVA and above
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: The binding regulation expresses the threshold as 63 kVA contracted demand (more liberal than the MoP rule's 100 kW). No threshold applies to captive consumers.

values.text: Wind WEGs commissioned in normal category pay 50% of conventional transmission/wheeling/scheduling charges (50% concession); REC-scheme WEGs pay 100% of conventional charges (0% concession)
values.raw_excerpt: Commission decides to retain levy of transmission, wheeling and scheduling and system operation charges at 50% of that applicable for conventional power notified by the Commission from time to time for the WEGs commissioned under the normal category. In respect of the WEGs availing Renewable Energy Certificates (REC), 100% of the respective charges as specified in the relevant orders of the Commission are applicable.
values.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 - Order on Procurement of Wind Power and Related Issues
values.caveat: Concession applies only to intra-state transactions; GEOA 2025 expressly bars concessions for inter-state/Power-Exchange green power. Concession level (50%) has continued since Order No. 6 of 2018.

values.text: Determined by SERC as 50% of the conventional wheeling/transmission charge per kVA/kW set in the prevailing TANGEDCO/TNPDCL tariff/OA-charges order, revised periodically
values.raw_excerpt: The energy losses for wheeling of energy shall be adjusted as per the relevant tariff order of the Commission. ... Charges for GEOA customers shall be as follows: 1. Transmission Charges; 2. Wheeling Charges ... the above charges including the concession except the charges on banking shall be as per the relevant Orders / Regulations of the Commission.
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: TNERC does not fix wheeling for wind as a flat INR/kWh; it is levied as 50% of the conventional wheeling charge per the periodically-revised distribution tariff order. Absolute INR/kWh value must be read from the current tariff order.

values.raw_excerpt: Charges for GEOA customers shall be as follows: 1. Transmission Charges; 2. Wheeling Charges; 3. Cross Subsidy Surcharge; 4. Additional Surcharge; 5. Banking Charges; 6. Standby charges ...
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: Cross-subsidy surcharge is levied on green-energy OA customers (effective 01-10-2025). CSS applies to intra-state transactions.

values.text: Wind third-party OA consumers pay 60% of conventional cross-subsidy surcharge (40% concession)
values.raw_excerpt: In the last tariff order for wind power, 60% of cross subsidy surcharge was levied. Commission proposed levy of 100% Cross Subsidy Surcharge ... Commission decides to retain levy of cross subsidy surcharge at 60% of that applicable to conventional power as ordered in the Order No.6 of 2018 dt.13.4.2018.
values.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 - Order on Procurement of Wind Power and Related Issues
values.caveat: Wind third-party OA consumers pay 60% of conventional CSS per Order No. 8 of 2020. Concession limited to intra-state transactions under GEOA 2025. CSS rates and the concession are periodically revised.

values.raw_excerpt: GEOA customers are liable for all relevant charges, including transmission and wheeling charges, cross-subsidy surcharge, additional surcharge, banking charges, standby charges ... Charges for GEOA customers shall be as follows: ... 4. Additional Surcharge
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: GEOA 2025 levies additional surcharge on green-energy OA. REPA flags contention that this conflicts with MoP GEOA Rules 2022 exemption where fixed charges are already collected — a live contention as of late 2025.

values.text: Generators bear actual transmission and wheeling line losses in kind, adjusted as per the relevant tariff order of the Commission, not a fixed statutory percentage
values.raw_excerpt: Line losses: The generators shall bear the actual line losses in kind as specified in the respective orders of the Commission issued from time to time. ... The energy losses for wheeling of energy shall be adjusted as per the relevant tariff order of the Commission.
values.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 (line losses) read with TNERC GEOA Regulations 2025 (Reg.14)
values.caveat: TN levies actual transmission/wheeling losses in kind (energy), not a fixed percentage; the applicable loss figure is published monthly by the licensee and in the prevailing tariff order. Marked 'estimated' because no independent second source verbatim corroborated the 'no fixed statutory %' characterization.

values.raw_excerpt: Banking facility shall be provided to the consumers availing intra-state Green Energy Open Access. The surplus energy from a 'Green Energy' Generating Station after setoff shall be banked with the Distribution Licensee. There shall be no facility of banking for third party power purchase/sale.
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: Banking allowed for intra-state captive/self-use GEOA only; NOT for third-party OA (effective 01-10-2025).

values.raw_excerpt: The Banking of green energy shall be permitted only on billing cycle basis of the Open Access consumer, which is a Calendar month. The banked energy shall be utilized within the same billing cycle. Provided that ... in the case of Wind Energy Generators commissioned on or before 31-03-2018 under normal or under REC scheme, banking period of 12 months from the 1st April to 31st March of the succeeding year shall be continue to be permitted
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: GEOA 2025 eliminated annual banking for new RE — now MONTHLY (calendar-month billing cycle, no carry-forward). EXCEPTION: legacy wind commissioned on/before 31-03-2018 retains the 12-month (annual) banking period. This record captures the default for new wind.

values.raw_excerpt: The Banking Charges shall be adjusted in kind @ 8% of the energy banked.
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: 8% in-kind banking charge (effective 01-10-2025) for new solar/wind/hybrid. Legacy pre-31-03-2018 wind continues at 14% in kind per Order No. 6 of 2018. Unutilised banked energy sold to DISCOM at 75% of applicable RE/bid tariff.

values.raw_excerpt: in the case of Wind Energy Generators commissioned on or before 31-03-2018 under normal or under REC scheme, banking period of 12 months from the 1st April to 31st March of the succeeding year shall be continue to be permitted ... along with the applicable banking charges as decided in the previous tariff orders, i.e. 14% in kind.
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: 14% in-kind banking charge for legacy wind commissioned on or before 31-03-2018 (continuing from Order No. 6 of 2018). Applies only to grandfathered pre-2018 WEGs.

values.raw_excerpt: There shall be no facility of banking for third party power purchase/sale.
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: Major change under GEOA 2025: banking is no longer available for third-party open access (only for captive/self-use). Effective 01-10-2025.

values.raw_excerpt: renewable energy generating stations, nuclear power stations, and run-of-river hydro stations [shall] be treated as must-run stations and should not be curtailed except for grid security reasons
values.source_name: TNERC (State Load Despatch Centre Functions and Accountability) Regulations, 2026 (read with TNERC Guidelines for Management of RE Curtailment for Wind and Solar Generation)
values.caveat: Must-run status for RE (incl. wind and wind-solar hybrid up to approved evacuation capacity) is established in TNERC's SLDC Regulations and RE curtailment guidelines. Citation is via secondary reporting (Mercom); the primary TNERC regulation PDF was not directly retrieved, hence 'estimated'. Curtailment permitted only for grid-security reasons.

values.text: Relaxed micro-siting norms (5D×7D relaxed to 3D×5D on farm periphery); modified/annual banking arrangements for repowered/refurbished/life-extended WEGs; conversion to wind-solar hybrid; support for PFC/REC/IREDA loans; exemption from open-access charges from date of approval to commissioning or 1.5 years (whichever shorter); development charge Rs 30 lakh/MW. Mandatory for WEGs >20 years old. Valid 22-08-2024 to 31-03-2030.
values.raw_excerpt: 10.Incentives The Policy is hereby providing a host of incentives to encourage WEGS to undertake the repowering / refurbishment projects. ... 10.1 Micro siting norms ... 5D X 7D norms shall be relaxed to 3D X 5D. ... 10.2 Annual Banking Arrangements ... this policy introduces modified banking arrangements for [WEGs that have completed 20 years; WEGs opting for repowering/refurbishment/life extension; and WEGs commissioned after 31-03-2018 opting in].
values.source_name: Tamil Nadu Repowering, Refurbishment and Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects-2024 (G.O.(Ms.) No.80, Energy Dept, 22-08-2024)
values.caveat: Repowering must raise annual generation >=1.25x (refurbishment >=1.1x); life-extension needs >=90% rated generation. TNGECL is the State Nodal Agency. Policy valid from 22-08-2024 to 31-03-2030.

values.text: No state land allotment scheme for wind. Land acquisition, lease renewal, evacuation system augmentation, and site development are the developer's cost, risk, and responsibility.
values.raw_excerpt: The dismantling and de-commissioning of existing WEGs, land acquisition, augmentation of the evacuation system, renewal of leases, renewal of approvals, etc. shall be at the cost, risk and responsibility of the Project Developer.
values.source_name: Tamil Nadu Repowering, Refurbishment and Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects-2024 (G.O.(Ms.) No.80, Energy Dept, 22-08-2024)
values.caveat: Current TN wind policy places land acquisition wholly on the developer. No government land-allotment or lease concession for wind exists under the Repowering Policy 2024. No separate active greenfield TN wind policy with state land provisioning was located.

values.raw_excerpt: Applications will be processed through a single-window system ... All the applications related to GEOA shall be submitted to the portal set up by the Central Nodal Agency (CNA) ... set up and operate a single window green energy open access system
values.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
values.caveat: Single-window GEOA via Central Nodal Agency portal routed to State Nodal Agency (SLDC for short-term; STU for medium/long-term). Repowering Policy 2024 also provides online single-window approval via TNGECL (SNA). Effective 01-10-2025.

values.raw_excerpt: Levelized tariff 3.17 Rs./kWh | AD benefit 0.33 Rs./kWh | Net tariff 2.84 Rs./kWh
values.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Tariff Framework for Procurement of Power from Wind Power Projects (Clause 4.12)
values.caveat: Section-62 generic levelized tariff applies ONLY to wind projects BELOW the 10 MW threshold; fixed for 25 years. Projects at/above 10 MW are procured via competitive bidding (no preferential tariff). Control period 06.06.2022 to 31.03.2027; revised periodically.

values.raw_excerpt: the distribution licensees shall continue to procure electricity from the eligible Wind Power Projects through competitive bidding under Section 63 of the Act ... For Wind Power Projects below the threshold limit of 10 MW, the Commission deemed it more appropriate to determine the tariff under Section 62 of the Act.
values.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.1.3)
values.caveat: Dual regime: competitive_bidding for projects >= 10 MW (Sec 63); preferential/project-specific generic tariff (Sec 62) for projects < 10 MW. Dominant mode for utility-scale is competitive bidding.

values.raw_excerpt: shall be eligible for wheeling of energy for Captive Use/ Third party sale under open access ... The Commission decides to promote the third-party sale /consumption of Wind Energy by allowing 25% concession in the Cross Subsidy Surcharge and Additional Surcharge
values.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.3, 2.6)
values.caveat: Permitted via Green Energy Open Access (>= 100 kW). Also per RE Policy 2023 Clause 9.1.3 / 10.4. Governed by GERC GEOA Regulations 2024.

values.raw_excerpt: Provided that there shall be no capacity restriction for setting up of RE projects for captive use with respect to the consumer's contract demand/sanctioned load (kW/MW, KVA/MVA) with Discoms.
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 1(2) proviso)
values.caveat: Captive use allowed with NO capacity restriction (the 100 kW GEOA floor does not apply to captive). Captive status must satisfy Electricity Rules 2005 ownership/consumption tests; failure attracts CSS + additional surcharge.

values.raw_excerpt: applicable for Green Energy Open Access (GEOA) ... Consumer who have contracted demand or sanctioned load of Hundred (100) KW or more, either through single connection or through multiple connections aggregating Hundred (100) kW or more located in same electricity division
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 1(2))
values.caveat: 100 kW floor for GEOA (single or aggregated connections in same division). No capacity restriction for captive use. Mirrors MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 (as amended).

values.text: Determined in distribution licensee's MYT/wires-business tariff order under Sec 64(3); not a flat Rs/kWh specified in the RE policy or Wind Tariff Order.
values.raw_excerpt: Wheeling charges payable to distribution licensee, by the Green Energy Open Access Consumer for usage of its system shall be as determined by the Commission in the tariff order from time to time ... Wheeling Charge for Green Energy Open Access shall be computed in Rs. /kWh and it shall be charged on the energy wheeled.
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.2)
values.caveat: The GEOA Regulations and Wind Tariff Order both DEFER the numeric wheeling charge (Rs/kWh) to the distribution licensee's wires-business/MYT tariff order under Sec 64(3); no fixed RE-specific figure is stated. A single flat numeric value cannot be pinned without the DISCOM-specific wheeling tariff order.

values.raw_excerpt: the Commission do not find any merit for allowing concessional transmission / wheeling chargers and losses as it will burden other consumers.
values.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.6.3 ruling)
values.caveat: No concession on wheeling/transmission charges or losses under the 2023 Policy / 2024 order (the concessions formerly granted under Wind Power Policy 2016 were withdrawn). Concession instead given only on CSS and Additional Surcharge.

values.raw_excerpt: The Green Energy Open Access facility availed by a consumer shall be required to pay cross subsidy surcharge as provided in relevant Tariff Orders issued by the Commission from time to time, in addition to transmission and/or wheeling charges.
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.3)
values.caveat: CSS applies to third-party-sale GEOA consumers; NOT levied on genuine captive consumption (Reg 17.3(2) proviso). Capped at 20% of Average Cost of Supply. Numeric per-unit CSS set in each DISCOM tariff order.

values.raw_excerpt: The Commission decides to promote the third-party sale /consumption of Wind Energy by allowing 25% concession in the Cross Subsidy Surcharge and Additional Surcharge wherein the RE generator and consumer does not claim RE attribute and allow distribution licensee to avail the same for RPO compliance.
values.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.6.3 (d)/(e))
values.caveat: 25% CSS+AS concession ONLY if the RE attribute is surrendered to the DISCOM for RPO; if generator/consumer keeps RE attribute for own RPO or project is under REC, then 100% CSS+AS applies (no concession). Separately, GEOA Reg 17.3 caps CSS escalation to +50% of grant-year value over 12 years.

values.raw_excerpt: The Green Energy Open Access consumers are required to pay the Additional Surcharge as determined by the Commission ... shall not be more than the per unit fixed cost of power purchase of the distribution licensee concerned.
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.4)
values.caveat: Applies to third-party GEOA (to extent OA exceeds contract demand); NOT applicable to captive use, nor to offshore wind commissioned up to Dec 2032. 25% concession available (see css_concession). Numeric value set per DISCOM order.

values.text: Transmission losses determined in distribution licensee's MYT/wires-business tariff order; not stated as a fixed percentage in the RE order.
values.raw_excerpt: The applicable rate of Transmission Charge and transmission losses payable by Green Energy Open Access Consumer shall be specified by the Commission in its Tariff Order ... In addition to Wheeling Charge, Wheeling loss shall be applicable ... as determined by the Commission in the tariff order from time to time.
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.1.2 / 17.2(b))
values.caveat: Both transmission losses and wheeling losses are deferred to the prevailing MYT/wires tariff order (applied on energy-fed-in basis); no fixed numeric loss % is stated in the RE Policy 2023, GEOA Regulations 2024, or Wind Tariff Order No. 5 of 2024. Cannot pin a single % without the GETCO/DISCOM tariff order.

values.raw_excerpt: Banking facility shall be permitted to the consumers availing Green Energy Open Access. ... The banking facility is an optional facility provided to the GEOA consumers.
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.6)
values.caveat: Optional; permitted quantum at least 30% of total consumption during billing period. Once opted, cannot change for 3 years.

values.raw_excerpt: The consumption of banked energy shall be permitted on billing cycle basis ... The credit for banked energy shall not be permitted to be carried forward to subsequent billing cycles and shall be adjusted during the same billing cycle ... the unutilized surplus banked energy shall be considered as lapsed at the end of each billing cycle
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.6 (iv),(xi))
values.caveat: Banking is on a billing-cycle (effectively monthly) basis; NO carry-forward to subsequent billing cycles; surplus lapses each billing cycle (generator then gets REC for lapsed energy). Peak-banked energy usable in peak+off-peak; off-peak-banked usable only in off-peak.

values.raw_excerpt: The Banking Charge at rate of Rs. 1.50 per unit shall be effective from the date of notification of these Regulations up to 30th September 2024. Thereafter, the banking charge ... as decided by the Commission and separately notified by the Regulation, shall be applicable.
values.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.6 (viii))
values.caveat: Rs 1.50/unit (a per-kWh charge, NOT a percentage). Originally effective to 30.09.2024 but repeatedly extended by successive GERC amendments (latest extension reported through 31.08.2026 pending a long-term methodology study). Confirm the current amendment for the exact effective date.

values.raw_excerpt: The Rules recommends Banking facility for the RE projects set up for captive use and third party [sale] ... Banking facility shall be permitted to the consumers availing Green Energy Open Access.
values.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 (sec on banking) read with GEOA Regulations 2024 (Reg 17.6)
values.caveat: Banking available to all GEOA consumers (third-party and captive) on payment of Rs 1.50/unit banking charge, subject to the 30%-of-consumption cap and no carry-forward.

values.raw_excerpt: 2025-26  Wind 1.45%  Hydro 1.22%  Distributed 2.10%  Other 28.24%  Total 33.01% ... Note 1: The wind renewable energy component shall be met by energy produced from Wind Power Projects (WPPs) commissioned after the 31st March, 2024.
values.source_name: GERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) Regulations, 2025, Notification No. 07 of 2025 (Table-1)
values.caveat: MAJOR DEFINITIONAL CHANGE: Under the current 2025 RPO Regulations (notified 12.08.2025), the 'Wind' RPO component (FY25-26=1.45%, FY26-27=1.97%, ... FY29-30=3.48%) counts ONLY wind commissioned AFTER 31.03.2024. Pre-April-2024 wind now falls under the 'Other' component (28.24% FY25-26). This is NOT comparable to the old single 'Wind RPO' of 8.55% (FY24-25) under the now-repealed GERC RPO (3rd Amendment) Regulations 2022.

values.source_name: GERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) Regulations, 2025, Notification No. 07 of 2025 (Table-1)
values.caveat: Total RPPO for FY2024-25. Supersedes the earlier 20.70% (FY24-25) figure from the repealed 2022 amendment.

values.source_name: GERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) Regulations, 2025, Notification No. 07 of 2025 (Table-1)
values.caveat: Total RPPO for FY2025-26, comprising Wind 1.45%, Hydro 1.22%, Distributed 2.10%, Other 28.24%. Verbatim from Gazette Extra No. 249 dated 12.08.2025.

values.raw_excerpt: The Indian Electricity Grid Code 2023 as well as State Grid Code provides 'Must Run' Status to Renewables, irrespective of the plant capacity and hence, RE plant shall not be subjected to Merit Order Despatch Principle.
values.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.5.3 ruling)
values.caveat: Must-run per IEGC 2023 / State Grid Code. However, for projects under competitive-bidding PPAs, curtailment/backing-down with compensation is permitted per MoP Bidding Guidelines dated 26/28.07.2023 and PPA terms.

values.text: Repowering is mandated as an obligation (not a financial incentive): old/inefficient wind turbine generators must be repowered on or before completion of 25 years from commissioning; failure requires decommissioning and surrender of connectivity and leasehold rights.
values.raw_excerpt: the repowering of wind turbine generators shall have to be done by the RE developers on or before the completion of 25 years from the date of commissioning of the project or extended term of the agreement. ... If the Wind Project developer fails to repower ... such RE developer shall have to decommission the wind power project and surrender the connectivity
values.source_name: Gujarat Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (Clause 29 - Repowering of wind projects)
values.caveat: RE Policy 2023 frames repowering as an OBLIGATION (re-energize old turbines, optimally use land), not a subsidy/tariff incentive. Life of repowered project = 25 years. No separate financial repowering incentive (e.g., extra tariff/GBI) is specified in the policy.

values.text: RE projects may be set up on private land or on government waste land allotted by Revenue Department/state nodal agency; government waste land may be allotted at concessional rates to projects supplying power for Gujarat consumers.
values.raw_excerpt: The RE project may be setup on private land, or government waste land allotted by the Revenue Department / state nodal agency. ... State Government may allocate government waste land at concessional rates to RE projects supplying power to the distribution licensee for the consumers of Gujarat.
values.source_name: Gujarat Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (Clause 28 - Land)
values.caveat: Concessional government-waste-land allotment is conditional on supplying power to DISCOM for Gujarat consumers; allotment 'based on optimum utilization of the entire land parcel'. Exact lease rates set by Revenue Dept, not fixed in the policy text.

values.text: No exemption. Electricity Duty governed by Gujarat Electricity Duty Act 1958 and its amendments from time to time (the earlier Wind Policy 2016 electricity-duty exemption was withdrawn).
values.raw_excerpt: Electricity Duty shall be governed in accordance with the provisions of the Gujarat Electricity Duty Act 1958 and its amendments from time to time.
values.source_name: Gujarat Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (Clause 18 - Electricity Duty)
values.caveat: RE Policy 2023 provides NO blanket electricity-duty exemption (a departure from Wind Power Policy 2016 which had granted one); duty is whatever the Gujarat Electricity Duty Act 1958 prescribes from time to time. Any duty relief would have to come from that Act/notifications, not this policy.

values.raw_excerpt: The SNA shall facilitate the RE Developers by developing a 'Single Window Web-System' for RE project. ... The registration completion and approval thereof shall be issued automatically through online-mode and made available on the web-portal itself.
values.source_name: Gujarat Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (Clause 25.3 - State Nodal Agency / Single Window)
values.caveat: Single-window web-system operated by the State Nodal Agency (GEDA) for registration/approval/allocation; GUVNL is implementing agency. Integrated with central single-window GEOA portal per MoP GEOA Rules 2022.

values.raw_excerpt: The generic tariff arrived at is Rs.3.24 per kWh for the purpose of Bidding for the Period FY2026-27 to FY2028-29 (down from Rs.3.34/kWh valid until 31.03.2026).
values.source_name: KERC Order - Generic Ceiling Tariff for Wind Power FY2026-27 to FY2028-29, dated 30/04/2026 (corrected per WindInsider verification)
values.caveat: This Rs.3.24/kWh is a GENERIC CEILING tariff (the actual claim of Rs.3.60 in the source was refuted by independent verification). Wind procurement is mandatorily via competitive e-reverse bidding, with this generic tariff acting only as the ceiling price; actual PPA tariffs are discovered via bidding and are typically lower. Prior ceiling was Rs.3.34/kWh (valid until 31.03.2026). Ceilings are revised periodically per KERC orders.

values.raw_excerpt: The Commission vide its Order dated 05.06.2018, mandated procurement of wind power by DISCOMs only through competitive e-reverse bidding, with the Generic Tariff determined by the Commission from time to time, as the ceiling price.
values.source_name: KERC Order dated 05.06.2018 / KERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) (9th Amendment) Regulations 2022
values.caveat: Wind procurement in Karnataka is mandatorily via competitive e-reverse bidding since the KERC Order dated 05.06.2018 and the 9th Amendment (2022) to the RE Procurement Regulations; the Commission's generic tariff functions only as the bidding ceiling, NOT as a preferential/cost-plus offtake tariff. The RE Policy 2022-27 likewise routes ESCOM procurement through competitive bidding.

values.raw_excerpt: The RE project developers can sell the energy to ESCOMs/Procurers, intermediaries or any consumer under open access (captive/group captive and third party sale including bulk consumers, through Power Exchanges) both within Karnataka and/or outside the State under this Policy.
values.source_name: Karnataka Renewable Energy Policy 2022-2027 (Notification No. ENERGY/209/VSC/2021, 16-04-2022), clause 5.2 Energy Sale
values.caveat: Permitted under the RE Policy 2022-27 and operationalised under KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (notified 26.03.2025), subject to open-access charges. Open-access eligibility requires >=100 kW contract demand/sanctioned load.

values.raw_excerpt: Provided further that, the Captive Consumers shall not have any load limit.
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (No. KERC-2-TR-2024-25/1805, dated 26.03.2025), definition of Open Access Consumer
values.caveat: Captive/group-captive use is explicitly permitted in both the RE Policy 2022-27 and the OA Regulations 2025. Captive consumers are exempt from the 100 kW open-access threshold and (per the OA regs) from CSS and additional surcharge. Captive status criteria as per Electricity Rules 2005.

values.raw_excerpt: (i) Shall have contract demand of 100kW and above in case of High Tension (HT) consumers (ii) Shall have sanctioned load of 100 kW and above in case of Low Tension (LT) consumers, either through single connection or through multiple connections for LT, aggregating to one hundred kW or more located in the same electricity division of a distribution licensee
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, definition of Open Access Consumer
values.caveat: Open-access eligibility threshold is 100 kW (contract demand for HT / sanctioned load for LT, LT aggregable within the same electricity division). This aligns with the MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 (50 kW) only partly - Karnataka applies 100 kW as the general OA threshold. Captive consumers have NO load limit. EV charging stations have special aggregation provisions.

values.raw_excerpt: Wheeling Charges: As determined by the Commission in Tariff Orders issued from time to time, in accordance with the MYT Regulations. [No renewable/wind-specific wheeling-charge concession is provided.]
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.b
values.caveat: The 2025 OA Regulations contain NO renewable/wind-specific wheeling-charge concession. The earlier concessions under the KERC Green Energy Open Access (GEOA) Regulations 2022 were struck down by the Karnataka High Court, and the new 2025 regulations apply standard wheeling charges to RE open-access transactions (no rebate clause). Value recorded as 0% concession under the current regime; verify against each ESCOM tariff order for any voltage-specific treatment.

values.raw_excerpt: Cross Subsidy Surcharge: The Cross-subsidy surcharge shall be as per the provisions of Tariff Policy notified by the Central Government under the Act. ... Provided further that the cross-subsidy surcharge shall not exceed 20% of the tariff applicable to the relevant category of consumer. Provided also that such cross-subsidy surcharge shall not be levied in case distribution access is provided to a person who has been availing power from the generation plant established as a captive plant for his own use.
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.c
values.caveat: CSS is applicable to third-party open-access consumers (computed per the Central Tariff Policy formula, capped at 20% of the applicable consumer tariff). CSS is NOT levied on captive/group-captive consumers, nor on power from non-fossil Waste-to-Energy plants. Actual per-unit CSS value is determined in ESCOM tariff orders and revised periodically.

values.raw_excerpt: Such cross-subsidy surcharge shall not be levied in case distribution access is provided to a person who has been availing power from the generation plant established as a captive plant for his own use.
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.c
values.caveat: No general percentage CSS concession for wind/RE third-party sale in the 2025 regs; CSS is capped at 20% of consumer tariff and NOT applied to captive use. Full CSS exemption applies only to captive use (self-use) and non-fossil Waste-to-Energy supply. Earlier GEOA-2022 RE concessions were struck down. Specific concession value could not be independently corroborated.

values.raw_excerpt: Additional Surcharge: ... (ii) Additional surcharge shall be on a per unit basis, payable monthly by open access customers based on the actual energy drawn under open access. (iii) Additional surcharge is not applicable in case distribution access is provided to a person who has been availing power from the generation plant established as a captive plant for his own use.
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.d
values.caveat: Additional surcharge applies to third-party open-access consumers (per-unit, determined annually on a stranded-cost basis via BESCOM petition). NOT applicable to captive consumers, nor to non-fossil Waste-to-Energy supply. The per-unit value is set in periodic orders.

values.raw_excerpt: Transmission Loss Trajectory approved for the Control Period | Target (Average) | FY2025-26 2.866 | FY2026-27 2.816 | FY2027-28 2.766
values.source_name: KERC Tariff Order 2025 (KPTCL) - Combined Tariff Order, dated 26.03.2025, Table-5.3
values.caveat: This is the approved INTRA-STATE TRANSMISSION loss target (average) for KPTCL: 2.866% (FY2025-26), 2.816% (FY2026-27), 2.766% (FY2027-28). Losses are applied 'in kind' for open-access transactions per OA Regs 2025 clause 12.g. DISTRIBUTION (wheeling) losses are separate, ESCOM- and voltage-specific, and set in each ESCOM tariff order (not a single statewide figure). Control period FY2025-26 to FY2027-28; revised each control period.

values.raw_excerpt: Monthly Banking shall be allowed for a period of five (5) years from the effective date, for those WBA entered, under these Regulations.
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.e(i)
values.caveat: Banking is allowed for OA/captive consumers sourcing from wind/solar/mini-hydel/hybrid, via a Wheeling & Banking Agreement, for 5 years from the effective date. NO banking facility for projects under the ISTS category (RE Policy 2022-27, clause c.2.3). Existing WBAs executed before 13.01.2023 (STOA)/02.01.2023 (LTOA) seeking renewal are not entitled to banking.

values.raw_excerpt: The unutilized banked energy (as applicable) remaining at the end of the month, shall not be permitted to be carried forward to subsequent months. However, the RE generating stations would be entitled to Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) for such energy remaining unutilized at the end of the month.
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.e(ii)
values.caveat: Banking is MONTHLY (settled slot-wise/day-wise and summated at month-end; unutilized energy is NOT carried to the next month - it converts to RECs). The 5-year figure is the duration the banking FACILITY is available, not the carry-forward period. The prior annual-banking regime under the older WBAs/GEOA was discontinued (the HC ruled there is no statutory right to banking).

values.raw_excerpt: The banking charges shall be 8% of the banked energy in kind or as determined by the Commission from time to time.
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.e(iii)
values.caveat: Banking charge is 8% of banked energy IN KIND (not cash). A Karnataka High Court interim arrangement (recited in the OA 2025 preamble) had directed 4% pending these regulations; the operative regulatory rate under the 2025 regs is 8%. Subject to revision by the Commission.

values.raw_excerpt: Banking means the facility by which electrical energy remaining unutilized by the OA Consumer or Captive Consumer sourcing power from wind, solar, mini-hydel... utilized for wheeling to such consumers for later use, as per the terms and conditions.
values.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, definitions
values.caveat: Banking is available to BOTH 'OA Consumer' (which includes third-party open-access consumers) and 'Captive Consumer' sourcing from wind/solar/mini-hydel/hybrid, via a Wheeling & Banking Agreement. Hence banking is available for third-party sale arrangements (intra-state), subject to the same 8% in-kind charge and monthly settlement. Not available for ISTS-category projects. Specific third-party-banking eligibility could not be independently corroborated from second sources.

values.raw_excerpt: Wind RE component 1.45% for FY2025-26. The wind renewable energy component shall be met by energy produced from Wind Power Projects (WPPs) commissioned after the 31st March, 2024.
values.source_name: KERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) (Eleventh Amendment) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. KERC/SC(F)/RPO/2024/1358, dated 21/01/2025)
values.caveat: Wind RPO is a distinct year-wise component aligned with GoI notification S.O.4617(E) dated 20-10-2023. Trajectory: 0.67% (FY24-25), 1.45% (FY25-26), 1.97% (FY26-27), 2.45% (FY27-28), 2.95% (FY28-29), 3.48% (FY29-30). Wind RE component must be met from WPPs commissioned AFTER 31-03-2024 (older WPPs count under 'Other RE'). Shortfall in Wind may be met by excess Hydro and vice versa.

values.raw_excerpt: Wind RE component 1.97% for FY2026-27. The wind renewable energy component shall be met by energy produced from Wind Power Projects (WPPs) commissioned after the 31st March, 2024.
values.source_name: KERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) (Eleventh Amendment) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. KERC/SC(F)/RPO/2024/1358, dated 21/01/2025)
values.caveat: Wind RPO is a distinct year-wise component aligned with GoI notification S.O.4617(E) dated 20-10-2023. Trajectory: 0.67% (FY24-25), 1.45% (FY25-26), 1.97% (FY26-27), 2.45% (FY27-28), 2.95% (FY28-29), 3.48% (FY29-30). Wind RE component must be met from WPPs commissioned AFTER 31-03-2024 (older WPPs count under 'Other RE'). Shortfall in Wind may be met by excess Hydro and vice versa.

values.raw_excerpt: Total RE (non-fossil) RPO: 33.01% for FY2025-26, comprised of Wind 1.45%, Hydro RE 1.22%, Distributed RE 2.10%, Other RE 28.24%.
values.source_name: KERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) (Eleventh Amendment) Regulations, 2025
values.caveat: Total RE (non-fossil) RPO trajectory: 29.91% (FY24-25), 33.01% (FY25-26), 35.95% (FY26-27), 38.81% (FY27-28), 41.36% (FY28-29), 43.33% (FY29-30). Applies to distribution licensees, captive and open-access consumers (as % of consumption from non-distribution-licensee sources). Excludes hydro commissioned before 01-04-2024 from the 'Hydro RE' sub-target.

values.raw_excerpt: Total RE (non-fossil) RPO: 35.95% for FY2026-27, comprised of Wind 1.97%, Hydro RE 1.34%, Distributed RE 2.70%, Other RE 29.94%.
values.source_name: KERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) (Eleventh Amendment) Regulations, 2025
values.caveat: Total RE (non-fossil) RPO trajectory: 29.91% (FY24-25), 33.01% (FY25-26), 35.95% (FY26-27), 38.81% (FY27-28), 41.36% (FY28-29), 43.33% (FY29-30). Applies to distribution licensees, captive and open-access consumers (as % of consumption from non-distribution-licensee sources). Excludes hydro commissioned before 01-04-2024 from the 'Hydro RE' sub-target.

values.raw_excerpt: The must-run status for renewable energy projects is accorded as per the Indian Electricity Grid Code (IEGC) and the Karnataka Electricity Grid Code (KEGC). Renewable energy curtailment cannot be routine or commercially motivated, and backing down events must be technically justified.
values.source_name: Indian Electricity Grid Code / Karnataka Electricity Grid Code; MoP/CERC must-run framework (secondary summary)
values.caveat: Wind/solar must-run status in Karnataka derives from the national IEGC and the Karnataka Electricity Grid Code (and MoP must-run directions), not from a stand-alone KERC numeric order. SLDC may curtail only for genuine grid-security/safety reasons (curtailment must be technically justified, with compensation per CERC). Could not locate a single Karnataka primary document stating 'must-run' verbatim; basis is the grid codes - hence confidence=estimated. Recommend citing the KEGC text directly for legal defensibility.

values.text: Repowering of wind turbines >=10 years old promoted per MNRE guidelines; pre-repowering 3-yr-average generation continues on existing PPA terms, additional generation purchased by ESCOMs at a tariff discovered through competitive bidding (subject to KERC approval), or used for captive/group-captive/third-party sale within/outside the State.
values.raw_excerpt: The Government of Karnataka will promote repowering of existing wind turbines which have completed at least 10 years in operation. Repowering shall comply with the Policy and guidelines of MNRE for repowering the wind power projects... the power generated corresponding to average of last three years' generation prior to repowering would continue to be procured on the terms of PPA in-force, as approved by the KERC and remaining additional generation may be purchased by ESCOMs as per their requirements at a tariff discovered through competitive bidding, subject to the approval of KERC. The repowered wind projects shall also be allowed to use the additional power for captive/group captive or third party sale within and/or outside the State.
values.source_name: Karnataka Renewable Energy Policy 2022-2027, clause 5.2.3 b.3 (Repowering of Existing Wind Energy Projects)
values.caveat: The policy enables/facilitates repowering but does NOT grant a specific fiscal subsidy or bonus tariff; the incentive is regulatory facilitation (continuation of in-force PPA for baseline generation + flexible offtake for additional generation). Aligned to MNRE repowering policy/guidelines.

values.text: Developer responsible to acquire/lease land; wind & wind-solar-hybrid projects allotted by the State Level Allotment Committee (SLAC) on KREDL recommendation; on NOC by SLAC/Energy Dept, agricultural land is treated as deemed converted (per Revenue Dept circulars); developers may begin execution on filing of conversion application with fees; RE projects treated as 'manufacturing industry' for incentives.
values.raw_excerpt: It shall be the responsibility of the RE project developer to acquire/lease the land required for the project development. ... Once the project is approved/issued NOC by SLAC/Energy Department, Government of Karnataka, and after following the procedures... the land required for the projects will be considered deemed converted. ... Allocation of land to solar and wind power projects shall be as per clause 6.2 of this Policy. The wind projects, solar-wind hybrid projects/parks... shall be approved by the State Level Allotment Committee chaired by the Additional Chief Secretary/Principal Secretary to Government, Energy Department.
values.source_name: Karnataka Renewable Energy Policy 2022-2027, clause 5.2 (b. Land) and clause 8.2 (SLAC)
values.caveat: No free/discounted government land grant for wind; land procurement is the developer's responsibility. The 'incentive' is procedural: deemed conversion of agricultural land on SLAC/Energy Dept NOC (via Revenue Dept circulars RD 01 LRM 2016 dated 03.02.2016 and RD/LGP/2015 dated 01.12.2015) and permission under s.109 Karnataka Land Reforms Act 1961 / KLR (Amendment) Act 2020. RE projects deemed manufacturing industry (excluding investment-promotion subsidies).

values.raw_excerpt: The project developer shall not require obtaining project approval (excluding applicable statutory approvals) from any other Government departments.
values.source_name: Karnataka Renewable Energy Policy 2022-2027, clause 5.2 b (Land - process of procurement and conversion)
values.caveat: The RE Policy 2022-27 contains NO Gram Panchayat NOC requirement for wind projects; approvals flow through SLAC/Energy Department and statutory clearances (KSPCB consent, forest/environment, etc.), and the policy expressly removes the need for approval from 'any other Government departments' beyond statutory approvals. Recorded as false on the basis of absence in the primary policy; local-body/panchayat building or land permissions may still arise under other statutes - verify case-by-case.

values.text: Not granted by the RE Policy itself; applicability of electricity duty is as per the direction of Government of Karnataka issued from time to time. RE projects are eligible for State Industrial Policy incentives (RE deemed manufacturing industry), which may include duty concessions.
values.raw_excerpt: Applicability of electricity duty to the RE projects developed under this Policy shall be as per the direction of Government of Karnataka issued from time to time.
values.source_name: Karnataka Renewable Energy Policy 2022-2027, clause 5.2 c.1 (Connectivity / charges)
values.caveat: RE Policy defers electricity-duty applicability to separate GoK direction; no blanket duty exemption is granted by the policy itself. RE projects are eligible for State Industrial Policy incentives (RE deemed manufacturing industry), which may include duty concessions via separate notifications. Secondary sources cite 100% duty exemption for 8 years, but this could not be verified against a primary Government notification.

values.raw_excerpt: A State Level Allotment Committee (SLAC) will be constituted for approval of the RE projects i.e., wind projects, wind-solar hybrid projects/parks and mini & small Hydro projects under this Policy and to fast track its implementation. ... KREDL as a State Nodal Agency shall carry out the following functions: 1. Verification and Recommendation of proposals to GoK. 2. Act as a facilitator to obtain all statutory clearances by issuing facilitation letters. ... The concerned department shall convey the approval/clearance/observation or comments if any within a stipulated time period of 45 days from the date of application.
values.source_name: Karnataka Renewable Energy Policy 2022-2027, clause 8.2 (State Level Allotment Committee) and KREDL nodal-agency functions
values.caveat: The policy provides a single-window-LIKE facilitation mechanism (SLAC for wind project allotment + KREDL as State Nodal Agency issuing facilitation letters, with a 45-day departmental response window), but the exact phrase 'single window' is NOT used verbatim in the RE Policy 2022-27. Treat as functional single-window clearance via SLAC/KREDL rather than a statutorily named single-window portal.

values.raw_excerpt: The State will promote setting up of Wind Power Projects for sale of power to DISCOMs of Rajasthan on the tariff discovered through competitive bidding process to fulfill Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO) target fixed by RERC.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 8.1) / RERC order Petition 1/2025 dated 01.08.2025
values.caveat: Rajasthan procures utility wind via competitive bidding (SECI/RUVNL); RERC does not set a generic preferential wind tariff. RERC order dated 01.08.2025 adopted the competitively-discovered SECI rate of Rs 2.44/kWh for a legacy PPA extension, confirming competitive bidding, not cost-plus preferential, as the operative mode. Also affirmed in Rajasthan Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (clause 12.1).

values.raw_excerpt: The State will promote setting up of Wind Power Projects for captive use/third party sale for consumers within the State. ... The generating plant capacity for third party sale can be any capacity, however the consumer will be allowed to take power from such plant up to the contract demand only.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 8.2.1)
values.caveat: Reaffirmed in Rajasthan Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (clause 12.2). Third-party sale requires open access compliance under RERC GEOA Regulations 2025; note banking is NOT allowed for third-party open-access plants under the 2025 GEOA regulation.

values.raw_excerpt: The State will promote setting up of Wind Power Projects for captive use/third party sale for consumers within the State. The maximum permissible capacity of individual plant for captive use will be limited to the contract demand of the consumer.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 8.2.1)
values.caveat: Reaffirmed in Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 12.2). Under RERC GEOA Regulations 2025, captive plants may be sized up to 200% of contract demand (BESS mandatory above 100%).

values.raw_excerpt: Entity means any consumer who has contracted demand or sanctioned load of one hundred kW or more either through single connection or through multiple connections aggregating one hundred kW or more located in the same electricity division of a distribution licensee, except for captive consumers. However, in case of captive consumers, there shall not be any load limitation.
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 2(f)), Order dated 21.05.2025, Petition 2264/2024
values.caveat: 100 kW threshold for non-captive entities; NO load limit for captive consumers. Final notified regulation (Order 21.05.2025). Regulations are revised periodically. URL is a republication of the RERC order; primary issuer is RERC (www.rerc.rajasthan.gov.in).

values.raw_excerpt: Wheeling Charges: EHV (132 kV and above) 0.01 Rs./kWh
values.source_name: RVPN/SLDC Charges payable for Open Access on Distribution System in Rajasthan FY2023-24 (RERC orders Petition 2066/2067/2068 of 2022 dated 31.03.2023), w.e.f. 15.06.2023
values.caveat: EHV (132 kV and above) rate. Voltage-differentiated: 33 kV = 0.15 Rs/kWh, 11 kV = 0.79 Rs/kWh. Latest official SLDC sheet is FY2023-24 (w.e.f. 15.06.2023); revised annually by RERC tariff orders. Prior FY2022-23 sheet had 0.01/0.16/0.77.

values.raw_excerpt: Wheeling Charges: 33 kV 0.15 Rs./kWh
values.source_name: RVPN/SLDC Charges payable for Open Access on Distribution System in Rajasthan FY2023-24 (RERC orders Petition 2066/2067/2068 of 2022 dated 31.03.2023), w.e.f. 15.06.2023
values.caveat: 33 kV rate. Voltage-differentiated charges. Latest official SLDC sheet is FY2023-24; revised annually by RERC tariff orders.

values.raw_excerpt: Wheeling Charges: 11 kV 0.79 Rs./kWh
values.source_name: RVPN/SLDC Charges payable for Open Access on Distribution System in Rajasthan FY2023-24 (RERC orders Petition 2066/2067/2068 of 2022 dated 31.03.2023), w.e.f. 15.06.2023
values.caveat: 11 kV rate. Voltage-differentiated charges. Latest official SLDC sheet is FY2023-24; revised annually by RERC tariff orders.

values.text: Storage-paired BESS projects with ≥5% BESS capacity exempt from 75-100% of transmission and wheeling charges for 7 years; green hydrogen/ammonia projects 50% intra-state T&W waiver to 2030.
values.raw_excerpt: RE Integrated Storage project with a capacity of 5% of RE capacity will be eligible for exemption of 75% on Transmission and Wheeling charges for a period of seven (7) years. ... For BESS beyond 30% of RE capacity, will be exempted from 100% Transmission & Wheeling charges.
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 11.3), Order dated 21.05.2025
values.caveat: The 50%/7yr wheeling concession for stand-alone wind captive/3rd-party in the 2019 Wind Policy (cl 34.5) EXPIRED in March 2023 / on reaching 500 MW. Current concessions under GEOA 2025 are conditional on BESS pairing or green hydrogen, NOT a blanket wind concession. Green H2/ammonia: 50% intra-state T&W waiver up to 2030.

values.raw_excerpt: The Green Energy Open Access facility availed by a consumer shall be required to pay cross subsidy surcharge as provided in relevant Tariff Order issued by the Commission ... Provided that such cross-subsidy surcharge shall not be levied in case green energy open access is provided to a person who has established a captive generation plant for carrying the electricity to the destination of his own use.
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 11.4), Order dated 21.05.2025
values.caveat: CSS applies to third-party green OA consumers but is NOT levied on captive consumers (self-use). Operative per-unit CSS values (FY2023-24): Non-Domestic 2.14-2.23, Mixed/Bulk 1.84-1.92, Large Industrial 1.58-1.67 Rs/unit by voltage (SLDC sheet, RERC Petition 2066-2068/2022). CSS capped at 20% of Average Cost of Supply.

values.raw_excerpt: the Commission may not increase cross-subsidy surcharge for Green Energy Open Access Consumer purchasing green energy ... during twelve years from the date of operating of the generating plant ... by more than fifty percent of the surcharge fixed for the year in which open access is granted
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 11.4 proviso), Order dated 21.05.2025
values.caveat: Not a flat 50% discount: CSS increase is capped at +50% of the surcharge fixed in the year OA was granted, for 12 years from commissioning. Separately, CSS shall not exceed 20% of Average Cost of Supply. Captive consumers are fully exempt from CSS.

values.raw_excerpt: The Additional Surcharge shall not be applicable to the Green Energy Open Access consumer for the quantum of Green Energy Open Access availed if the fixed charge is being paid by such Green Energy Open Access consumer to the distribution licensee ... up to contract demand / sanctioned load.
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 11.5), Order dated 21.05.2025
values.caveat: Additional surcharge is leviable in principle (operative value FY2023-24: 0.89 Rs/kWh per SLDC sheet) but NOT applicable to captive OA, nor where fixed charge is paid up to contract demand, nor for offshore wind commissioned up to Dec 2032, nor for green H2/ammonia. Capped at per-unit fixed cost of power purchase of the discom.

values.raw_excerpt: C. Transmission Losses (Petition no. RERC/2075/23 dated 08.06.2023) 4.5%
values.source_name: RVPN/SLDC Charges payable for Open Access on Transmission System in Rajasthan FY2023-24 (RERC Petition 2075/23 dated 08.06.2023), w.e.f. 15.06.2023
values.caveat: Intra-state transmission loss = 4.5% (FY2023-24); prior FY2022-23 was 3.80%. Distribution/wheeling system losses are separate and voltage-wise: 33 kV = 3.80%, 11 kV = 12.60% (FY2023-24). Revised annually by RERC; GEOA Reg 2025 (cl 15) defers losses to RERC as specified from time to time.

values.raw_excerpt: Banking of Energy subject to a maximum ceiling of 25 % of the energy injected by Renewable Energy Captive Generating Station during the month or 30% of the total monthly consumption of electricity from the distribution licensee by the consumer, whichever is higher, at consumption end shall be allowed only for captive consumption within the State.
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 12), Order dated 21.05.2025
values.caveat: Banking allowed only for captive consumption within the State (max ceiling 25% of monthly injection or 30% of monthly consumption, whichever higher; up to 200% CD with higher 30% ceiling on billing-cycle basis). The earlier 2019 Wind Policy allowed banking for captive AND third-party at 10% in-kind; that is superseded for GEOA consumers by the 2025 regulation.

values.raw_excerpt: Provided also that the banking shall be allowed on annual basis. ... Unutilized banked energy at the end of financial year shall lapse and the renewable energy captive generating plant shall be entitled to get Renewable Energy Certificates to the extent of the lapsed banked energy.
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 12), Order dated 21.05.2025
values.caveat: Annual (financial-year) basis for plants up to 100% CD; monthly set-off within the year, unused energy lapses at FY-end (with RECs for lapsed energy). For plants 100%-200% of CD, banking is on billing-cycle (monthly) basis. Banking year April-March per the 2019 policy lineage.

values.raw_excerpt: Banking charges at the rate of 8% of banked energy would be payable in kind and shall be adjusted against the banked energy before withdrawal ... If 100 Units of energy have been banked at the consumption end after accounting for losses, the Captive consumer will be able to draw 92 units of banked energy and 8 units will be deducted as banking charges.
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 12), Order dated 21.05.2025
values.caveat: 8% of banked energy, payable in kind (Commission may alter via separate order). This supersedes the 10% in-kind banking charge in the 2019 Wind Policy (cl 34.3) for GEOA consumers.

values.raw_excerpt: Provided that no banking facility shall be allowed for Renewable Energy plants supplying power to third party under open access and for the Renewable Energy plant installed behind the meter.
values.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 12), Order dated 21.05.2025
values.caveat: Conflict to flag: the 2019 Wind Policy (cl 34.3) permitted banking for both captive AND third-party sale at 10% in-kind. The current RERC GEOA Regulations 2025 expressly DISALLOW banking for third-party open access (and behind-the-meter). The 2025 regulation governs.

values.raw_excerpt: S.No. Year Wind RPO | 1 2024-25 2.46%
values.source_name: RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 [DRAFT] (Reg 4), amending RERC RPO Regulations 2023
values.caveat: DRAFT amendment (public comments due 23.03.2026; not yet finalized). The claimed figures EXACTLY match the primary draft PDF table. But this is a DRAFT (First Amendment) Regs 2026, effective only from FY2026-27 onward and NOT YET NOTIFIED. Per instruction, draft trajectories are not in force -> estimated until final notification.

values.raw_excerpt: S.No. Year Wind RPO | 2 2025-26 3.36%
values.source_name: RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 [DRAFT] (Reg 4), amending RERC RPO Regulations 2023
values.caveat: DRAFT amendment (not yet notified). Draft figures match the primary PDF exactly, but are not in force until final notification. Secondary source misreported wind as 0.81%/1.6%; the primary PDF supports the claimed 2.46%/3.36%/3.48%.

values.raw_excerpt: Wind 3.48% by FY2029-30
values.source_name: RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 [DRAFT] (Reg 4), amending RERC RPO Regulations 2023
values.caveat: DRAFT amendment trajectory to FY2029-30; not yet finalized or in force.

values.raw_excerpt: S.No. Year Total RPO | 1 2024-25 29.91%
values.source_name: RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 [DRAFT] (Reg 4), amending RERC RPO Regulations 2023
values.caveat: DRAFT amendment (comments due 23.03.2026). Total RPO figures match the primary draft PDF table exactly, but are DRAFT not yet notified.

values.raw_excerpt: S.No. Year Total RPO | 2 2025-26 33.01%
values.source_name: RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 [DRAFT] (Reg 4), amending RERC RPO Regulations 2023
values.caveat: DRAFT amendment. Figures match primary PDF; not yet in force.

values.raw_excerpt: Total renewable energy 43.33% by FY2029-30
values.source_name: RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 [DRAFT] (Reg 4), amending RERC RPO Regulations 2023
values.caveat: DRAFT trajectory to FY2029-30. Total RPO/RCO trajectory aligns with MoP: 29.91% (FY24-25) to 43.33% (FY29-30). From FY2026-27 onward governed by MoP/Central Govt RCO notifications.

values.raw_excerpt: SLDC will ensure must run Status of RE Plants in the State and maintain the data of RE Power Curtailment in transparent manner.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 33) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 33)
values.caveat: Must-run status affirmed in both the 2019 Wind Policy and the 2023 RE Policy; also consistent with RERC RE Tariff Regulations 2020 treating wind/solar/hybrid as must-run, and MoP's must-run rules. Curtailment monitoring by SLDC.

values.text: Repowering promoted for turbines >=10 years old; additional generation usable for captive/third-party sale or sold to DISCOMs at competitively-bid tariff; land lease extended for useful life of project.
values.raw_excerpt: The State will promote Repowering of existing wind turbines that have completed at least 10 years in operation. ... remaining additional generation may be purchased by DISCOMs at a tariff discovered through competitive bidding ... The Wind Power Producer shall also be allowed to use the additional generated power for captive use/third party sale.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 9) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 12.3)
values.caveat: No direct financial subsidy for repowering; incentives are: (a) baseline generation continues under existing PPA, surplus at competitively-bid tariff or for captive/3rd-party use; (b) land-lease extension for project's useful life; (c) under GEOA 2025, repowered+storage projects qualify for the BESS-linked T&W exemptions. The 2019 Wind Policy's 25% T&W concession for repowered captive plants (cl 34.5) expired Mar 2023.

values.text: Government land up to 3 hectares/MW under RE Land Allotment Rules 2007; Rs 1 lakh/MW cash security deposit; private agricultural land allowed without conversion.
values.raw_excerpt: For setting up of Wind Power Project, maximum area that may be allotted to the Developer shall be 3 hectares/MW ... only on submission of cash security deposit of Rs.1 Lac per MW ... Developer shall be permitted to setup Wind Power Project on private agriculture land without the requirement of land conversion.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 13) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 18)
values.caveat: Governed by Rajasthan Land Revenue (Allotment of Land for Setting up of Power Plant based on RE Sources) Rules, 2007 as amended. 3 ha/MW cap confirmed in both 2019 Wind Policy (cl 13.1.2) and RE Policy 2023 (cl 18.3). Security deposit Rs 1 lakh/MW (allotment) plus Rs 5 lakh/MW project security after in-principle clearance. Stamp duty = twice the agricultural rate.

values.raw_excerpt: NOC from Gram Panchayat will not be required for allotment of Government land (Siwai Chak land) for establishment of wind monitoring station.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 19.3) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 12.5.3)
values.caveat: The explicit Gram Panchayat NOC waiver in both policies is NARROW — it applies only to government (Siwai Chak) land allotment for a wind MONITORING STATION, not to the wind power project itself. The policies do not state a blanket project-level Gram Panchayat NOC waiver. Value 'false' reflects this limited scope; do not over-generalize. The broad 'no GP NOC required' reading and cited 2019 cl 19.3 could not be fully corroborated.

values.text: Electricity consumed for captive use of wind/wind-solar hybrid within the State exempted from Electricity Duty (per GoR orders under Rajasthan Electricity Duty Act / RIPS). Separately, 100% exemption for 10 years for wind-equipment manufacturing units.
values.raw_excerpt: The electricity consumed by the Power Producer of wind/wind-solar hybrid projects for captive use within State will be exempted from payment of Electricity Duty.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 34.4) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 19.4)
values.caveat: Two distinct exemptions: (1) GENERATION side - captive-use electricity from wind/hybrid exempt from electricity duty (2019 cl 34.4; 2023 cl 19.4 routes it via RIPS and GoR notifications under the Rajasthan Electricity (Duty) Act). (2) MANUFACTURING side - 'Full exemption in Electricity Duty for 10 years' (2019 cl 20) applies ONLY to wind-equipment manufacturers, NOT to power projects. Many secondary sources wrongly conflate these.

values.raw_excerpt: RREC will develop an online portal for sanction/approval of the proposals of RE [projects] ... RREC will work for simplification of various approvals required for setting up of RE facility in State, as a step towards ease of doing business.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (clauses 21.5 & 36)
values.caveat: Not a full statutory single-window clearance. The RE Policy 2023 provides an RREC online portal (cl 21.5) and committee-based clearance (SLSC/SLMCC), plus an 'ease of doing business' commitment (cl 36). Distinct from the state's general industrial single-window (One-Stop-Shop under Rajasthan investment law). Treat as 'partial/RREC-portal', not a guaranteed single-window.

values.text: No per-kWh green energy cess on wind generation; RREDF contribution applies only to the solar capacity of hybrid projects sold to non-DISCOM parties.
values.raw_excerpt: In case of Hybrid Power Project set up in Rajasthan for sale of power to parties other than DISCOMs of Rajasthan, a contribution towards Rajasthan Renewable Energy Development Fund shall be made by the power producer, on the solar capacity of such project ... Rs 2 lac/MW/year ... Rs 5 lac/MW/year.
values.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 29, RREDF)
values.caveat: No per-kWh 'green energy cess' on wind generation exists in the policies or RERC regulations reviewed. The nearest analogous levy is the RREDF contribution (Rs 2-5 lakh/MW/year, escalating by COD year) but it is charged ONLY on the SOLAR capacity of HYBRID projects sold to non-DISCOM parties; it is NOT levied on standalone wind, nor on captive solar within state, nor on sales to Rajasthan DISCOMs. green_energy_cess (Rs/kWh) for wind is effectively nil/not applicable.

values.raw_excerpt: Thus, for all the above RE Technologies, the Commission will not be determining the Generic Tariff and will only adopt the tariff discovered through transparent competitive bidding process as has been specified in the RE Tariff Regulations, 2019.
values.source_name: MERC Generic Renewable Energy Tariff Order for FY 2025-26, Case No. 1/SM/2025 (under MERC RE Tariff Regulations, 2019)
values.caveat: Per Reg 7.1 of MERC RE Tariff Regulations 2019, wind is one of the RE technologies whose tariff is 'invariably' determined through competitive bidding; MERC does NOT determine a generic/preferential wind tariff. Regulations extended to Second Review Period FY2025-26 to FY2029-30.

values.raw_excerpt: In case a Generating Station or a Licensee has accessed the Distribution System for sale of power to a Consumer eligible for Open Access or a Licensee or on the Power Exchange in accordance with these Regulations, the bill for use of the Distribution System for wheeling of electricity shall be raised on such Generating Station or Licensee...
values.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) Regulations, 2016, Reg 3.2 & 14.3
values.caveat: Third-party sale via open access is permitted. Wheeling charges, CSS and (where applicable) additional surcharge apply to such third-party OA transactions.

values.raw_excerpt: Subject to the provisions of these Regulations, a Consumer having Contract Demand of 1 MW and above with a Distribution Licensee shall be eligible for Open Access for obtaining supply of electricity from one or more a) Generating Plants or Stations, including Captive Generating Plants; ... Second Amendment 2023 expressly removed any load limitation for captive consumers ('in case of captive consumers, there shall not be any load limitation').
values.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) Regulations, 2016, Reg 3.2; Second Amendment 2023 (no load limit for captive)
values.caveat: Captive use via open access is permitted. Second Amendment 2023 expressly removed any load limitation for captive consumers. ~90% of Maharashtra's OA/captive RE consumption is via the captive route (RE Policy 2025).
