values.raw_excerpt: Consumers having Contract Demand or Sanctioned Load of 100 kW or more or as may be amended in the Rules from time to time, or Entity through multiple connections aggregating 100 kW or more located in same electricity circle of a Distribution Licensee, shall be eligible to take power from Green Energy through Open Access; Provided that there shall be no limit of supply of power for the captive consumers taking power under Green Energy Open Access
values.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2023 (Maharashtra Gazette, notified 10 November 2023), reproduced in MSEDCL Commercial Circular No. 346
values.caveat: Threshold for Green Energy Open Access reduced from 1 MW to 100 kW by the Second Amendment 2023. No threshold applies to captive consumers.

values.raw_excerpt: HT (Excl EHV) wheeling charge Rs/kVAh: 0.62 [FY2025-26, Table 437: Voltage-wise wheeling cost for 5th Control Period as approved by the Commission]
values.source_name: MERC MSEDCL MYT Order, Case No. 217 of 2024 (5th Control Period FY2025-26 to FY2029-30), Table 437, para 7.34.16
values.caveat: Approved HT (excl EHV) wheeling charge for MSEDCL is Rs 0.62/kVAh for FY2025-26, declining to Rs 0.53/kVAh by FY2029-30. Values are MSEDCL-specific and revised each MYT control period; other licensees (Tata, Adani, BEST) differ.

values.text: No general wind/RE concession; 10% discount only for Data Centres and semiconductor units consuming 100% green energy; EHV consumers pay zero wheeling charge (akin to a full rebate).
values.raw_excerpt: The Commission has approved discount of 10% in Wheeling Charges to such Data Centres and semi-conductor units.
values.source_name: MERC MSEDCL MYT Order, Case No. 217 of 2024, para 7.34.19
values.caveat: There is NO general wheeling-charge concession for wind/RE open access in Maharashtra. The only wheeling-charge discount in the current MYT order is 10% for Data Centres and semiconductor units consuming 100% green energy.

values.raw_excerpt: Cross-Subsidy Surcharge IS applicable to open-access consumers (incl. wind OA). For HT-Industry (typical wind OA buyer) the approved CSS for FY2025-26 is Rs 2.11/kWh, capped at 20% of Average Cost of Supply per National Tariff Policy 2016 formula.
values.source_name: MERC MSEDCL MYT Order, Case No. 217 of 2024, Section 7.35 & Table 441
values.caveat: CSS IS applicable to open-access consumers (incl. wind OA). For HT-Industry the approved CSS for FY2025-26 is Rs 2.11/kWh, capped at 20% of ACoS. CSS is exempt only where green energy is used for green hydrogen/ammonia or sourced from non-fossil Waste-to-Energy (Second Amendment 2023).

values.text: No general wind/RE concession; CSS capped at 20% of ACoS; full exemption only for WtE / green-H2 / green-NH3.
values.raw_excerpt: Cross-subsidy surcharge shall not be applicable in case power produced from a non-fossil fuel based Waste-to-Energy plant is supplied to the Open Access Consumer. Cross-subsidy surcharge shall not be applicable if green energy is utilised for production of green hydrogen and green ammonia.
values.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2023, amendment to Reg 14.7(d), via MSEDCL Commercial Circular No. 346
values.caveat: No across-the-board CSS concession for wind. CSS is statutorily capped at 20% of Average Cost of Supply (NTP 2016). Full CSS exemption applies only to specified cases: non-fossil Waste-to-Energy supply, and green energy used for green hydrogen/green ammonia.

values.raw_excerpt: Hence, the Commission is not approving the claim to levy of additional surcharge during the 5th Control Period.
values.source_name: MERC MSEDCL MYT Order, Case No. 217 of 2024, para 7.36.28
values.caveat: MERC did NOT approve any additional surcharge for MSEDCL for the 5th Control Period (FY2025-26 to FY2029-30). Marked 'estimated' because independent second-source confirmation for FY2025-26 specifically was not found, though the primary MYT Order para 7.36.28 is decisive.

values.raw_excerpt: Transmission Losses (%): 3.28% [FY 2025-26, CSS computation]; Intra-State Transmission Loss: 3.38% [FY 2024-25, para 13.7.30]
values.source_name: MERC MSEDCL MYT Order, Case No. 217 of 2024 (Section 7.35 CSS computation; Section 3.4/4 InSTS loss)
values.caveat: Intra-State Transmission (InSTS) loss approved ~3.28% for FY2025-26 CSS gross-up, declining toward 3.16% by FY2029-30. Wheeling (distribution) losses are separate: 7.5% at HT and 12% at LT. Losses are settled in-kind per DOA Reg 19.

values.raw_excerpt: The surplus energy from a 'non-firm' Renewable Energy Generating Station after set-off shall be banked with the Distribution Licensee. Banking of energy shall be permitted during all twelve months of the year.
values.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) Regulations, 2016, Reg 20
values.caveat: Banking of surplus RE is allowed, but subject to TOD-slot matching rules and lapse at end of each banking cycle (Second Amendment 2023).

values.raw_excerpt: Unused banked energy lapses monthly. Banking operates on monthly + TOD-slot basis within the year. Second Amendment 2023 added that unutilised surplus banked energy 'shall be considered as lapsed at the end of each banking cycle' (= monthly).
values.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2023; verified via K&K Advocates and Open Access Exchange independent sources
values.caveat: VERIFICATION CORRECTED: The claimed 'annual' banking period (from DOA 2016 Reg 20.3) has been REFUTED by the DOA Second Amendment 2023 and independent verification. Current banking period is MONTHLY, not annual. Unused banked energy lapses each month per the 2023 amendment.

values.raw_excerpt: Banking charges shall be adjusted in kind @ 8% of the energy banked.
values.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2023 (notified 10 November 2023), substituting Reg 20.5/20.4 of DOA Regulations 2016; via MSEDCL Commercial Circular No. 346
values.caveat: The Second Amendment 2023 substituted the original DOA Regulations 2016 (Reg 20.5) banking charge from 2% in-kind to 8% in-kind. The current rate is 8%.

values.raw_excerpt: Banking is available to non-firm RE generating stations supplying OA consumers (both captive and third-party), with no clause restricting banking to captive-only.
values.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) Regulations, 2016, Reg 20 (banking available to non-firm RE OA consumers generally)
values.caveat: Banking under Reg 20 is available to non-firm RE generating stations supplying OA consumers (both captive and third-party). Marked 'estimated' because the regulation does not contain an explicit standalone sentence stating 'third-party banking is allowed' — it is inferred from the general non-firm RE banking entitlement.

values.source_name: MERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation, its Compliance and Implementation of REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024 (Maharashtra Gazette, notified 23 February 2024), Reg 7.5(A)
values.caveat: Wind RPO introduced for the first time in the First Amendment 2024, applies only to energy from Wind Power Projects commissioned AFTER 31 March 2024.

values.source_name: MERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation, its Compliance and Implementation of REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024 (Maharashtra Gazette, notified 23 February 2024), Reg 7.5(A)
values.caveat: Wind RPO trajectory specified through FY2029-30. Shortfall in Wind RPO may be met with excess HPO and vice versa.

values.source_name: MERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation, its Compliance and Implementation of REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024 (Maharashtra Gazette, notified 23 February 2024), Reg 7.5(A)
MERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation, its Compliance and Implementation of REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024 (Maharashtra Gazette, notified 23 February 2024), Reg 7.5(A)
MERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation, its Compliance and Implementation of REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024 (Maharashtra Gazette, notified 23 February 2024), Reg 7.5(A)
MERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation, its Compliance and Implementation of REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024 (Maharashtra Gazette, notified 23 February 2024), Reg 7.5(A)
MERC (RPO and REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024, Reg 7.5(A)
values.caveat: Total RPO = Wind RPO + HPO + Distributed RPO + Other RPO.

values.source_name: MERC (RPO and REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024, Reg 7.5(A)
values.caveat: FY2025-26 Total = 33.01% (Wind 1.45% + HPO 1.22% + Distributed 2.10% + Other 28.24%).

values.source_name: MERC (RPO and REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024, Reg 7.5(A)
MERC (RPO and REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024, Reg 7.5(A)
MERC (RPO and REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024, Reg 7.5(A)
MERC (RPO and REC Framework) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2024, Reg 7.5(A)
values.caveat: Operating period of RPO framework runs to 31 March 2030.

values.raw_excerpt: Subject to the provisions of the Indian Electricity Grid Code and the State Electricity Grid Code, all RE Power Projects shall be treated as 'Must Run' Projects and shall not be subjected to 'merit order despatch' principles.
values.source_name: MERC (Terms and Conditions for Determination of Renewable Energy Tariff) Regulations, 2019, Reg 13.3
values.caveat: All RE projects (including wind) are 'Must Run' and not subject to merit-order despatch. RE Tariff Regulations 2019 extended to FY2029-30.

values.text: For projects selling power to MSEDCL through competitive bidding route, repowering projects shall be eligible for an additional Government of Maharashtra incentive payment of Rs 0.5/kWh for the first 5 years from the date of CoD. For projects set up for captive consumption, repowering projects shall be eligible for Electricity Duty exemption for the first 10 years from the date of CoD.
values.raw_excerpt: For projects selling power to MSEDCL through competitive bidding route, repowering projects shall be eligible for an additional Government of Maharashtra incentive payment of Rs 0.5/kWh for the first 5 years from the date of CoD. For projects set up for captive consumption, repowering projects shall be eligible for Electricity Duty exemption for the first 10 years from the date of CoD.
values.source_name: Maharashtra Renewable Energy & Energy Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36 (Industries, Energy, Labour & Mining Dept, GoM, March 2026), Section 9.4
values.caveat: Target of 1 GW wind repowering by 2030. Policy notified March 2026, valid to 31 March 2036; supersedes the Unconventional Energy Generation Policy 2020 for new projects.

values.text: Government land tiered (Gram Panchayat <=15 acres, Panchayat Samiti 15-25, Zilla Parishad >25); private land minimum lease rate Rs 1,25,000/hectare/year with 3% annual escalation; Non-agricultural tax/premium waived.
values.raw_excerpt: While seeking required private land on lease for RE (wind and solar) projects the base annual lease rate shall be as may be mutually agreed or the higher of: 6% of the land value determined for the year by the Registration and Stamps Department, or Rs 1,25,000 per hectare. This base lease rate shall increase by 3% annually using a flat escalation method. Non-agricultural tax / premium shall be waived for renewable energy and energy storage projects established under this policy.
values.source_name: Maharashtra Renewable Energy & Energy Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36, Sections 6.1-6.3
values.caveat: Government land categorized into 3 types with department-wise allotment routes. Private land minimum lease rate Rs 1.25 lakh/ha/yr with 3% escalation. NA-tax/premium waived.

values.raw_excerpt: For land held by local self-government bodies: If the land required for the project is up to 15 acres, it may be made available at the Gram Panchayat level. For land between 15–25 acres, it may be made available at the Panchayat Samiti level. For land exceeding 25 acres, it may be made available at the Zilla Parishad level.
values.source_name: Maharashtra Renewable Energy & Energy Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36, Section 6.1
values.caveat: The current RE Policy 2025 does NOT impose a standalone Gram Panchayat NOC requirement; rather, for local-body land up to 15 acres, the allotment authority is the Gram Panchayat. Marked 'estimated' because the policy describes an allotment hierarchy, not an explicit NOC mandate/exemption.

values.text: 100% exemption for 10 years from CoD for long-term captive Green OA projects integrating energy storage (minimum 4 hours of 50% of RE capacity contracted) and for captive repowering projects.
values.raw_excerpt: Long-term Captive Green OA projects in Maharashtra which supply power within Maharashtra which integrate energy storage (for a minimum of 4 hours of 50% of the RE capacity contracted) into the projects shall be exempt from payment of Electricity Duty for 10 years from the commissioning date.
values.source_name: Maharashtra Renewable Energy & Energy Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36, Sections 7.2.2 & 9.4(b)
values.caveat: Electricity Duty exemption (10 years from CoD) is conditional: targets long-term Captive Green OA projects integrating storage (>=4hr of 50% of RE capacity) supplying within Maharashtra, and captive repowering projects. Levied under the Maharashtra Electricity Duty Act, 2016.

values.raw_excerpt: This must ensure single window clearance of all required aspects of seeking Green OA for all eligible consumers in a time-bound manner as stipulated in the Rules and Regulations.
values.source_name: Maharashtra Renewable Energy & Energy Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36, Section 7.2.1 (also Central Nodal Agency single-window system per Second Amendment 2023, Reg 2.1(7)(a))
values.caveat: Single-window clearance is provided for Green OA via integrated SLDC/STU/DISCOM portals interfacing with the National Green Open Access Registry.

values.raw_excerpt: the generic rate approved by KSERC for wind energy projects without the benefit of accelerated depreciation ... CV Renewables ultimately accepted the Rs 3.94/unit generic tariff
values.source_name: KSERC Interim Order (Mar 2025) on CV Renewables 250 kW wind generator, Ramakkalmedu (generic wind tariff under KSERC RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020)
values.caveat: Verified as the generic wind tariff under KSERC RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 via a secondary report of the KSERC interim order dated 20-Mar-2025; primary order PDF not located. This is a PPA ceiling matching the generic tariff, not a distinct preferential feed-in incentive. Note: under the newer 2025 Regulations (currently stayed by Kerala HC as of 10-Nov-2025), wind FiTs are metering-specific (net metering Rs 2.87/kWh, net billing Rs 4.15/kWh, gross metering Rs 4.20/kWh) and IPP generic tariff is project-specific by wind zone CUF. Revisit after HC resolution.

values.raw_excerpt: 45.1 Tariff Based Competitive Bidding (TBCB) under Section 63 of the Act shall be the preferred mode for power procurement by the Distribution licensees for all RE sources ... The norms are also applicable to IPPs seeking generic or project specific tariff
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 45.1), No. 3228/Con.Engg/2023/KSERC dated 5-Nov-2025
values.caveat: TBCB is the PREFERRED mode; generic and project-specific tariffs under Sec 62 remain available for distribution-licensee projects and for IPPs where TBCB is not followed (with justification). Control period FY2025-26 to FY2029-30. The 2025 Regulations are STAYED by Kerala High Court (order dated 10-Nov-2025, next hearing 1-Dec-2025) — operation/implementation/enforcement halted; the prior RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 framework remains the last unstayed regime.

values.raw_excerpt: 34.1 IPPs shall have the right to open access for transmitting and/or wheeling renewable energy through the transmission and distribution system ... 34.2 A consumer purchasing power from an IPP supplying power to a third party by availing open access shall pay to the licensee the following charges
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 34 – Independent Renewable Power Generator); also RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Reg 22(1)(iii))
values.caveat: Third-party sale by an IPP via open access is expressly permitted in both the 2025 regs (Reg 34) and the 2020 regs (Reg 22(1)(iii): 'Renewable energy system installed by a Renewable Energy Generator as an Independent power producer, for third party sale'). Subject to OA charges. The 2020-regs basis is independently in force.

values.raw_excerpt: 33.1 Captive RE consumer(s) is the end user(s) of the RE generated in a Captive RE Generating Plant, established in accordance with the requirements specified in Rule 3 of 'the Electricity Rules, 2005' ... Provided that no cross-subsidy surcharge shall be applicable for the Captive consumer.
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 33 – Captive RE consumer); also RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Chapter IV)
values.caveat: Captive use is permitted in both frameworks; captive RE consumers are exempt from cross-subsidy surcharge but pay transmission/wheeling charges, losses and grid support charges. The 2020-regs captive provisions are independently in force.

values.raw_excerpt: 20.1(ii) Open Access shall be granted as per the provisions under KSERC (Connectivity and Intra State Open Access) Regulations, 2013 to consumers, who require a supply of electricity where the maximum power to be made available at any time exceeds 100 kW
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 20.1(ii))
values.caveat: Open access for RE is granted to consumers whose maximum power exceeds 100 kW, aligned with the MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 threshold. The 2020 regs used a 1 MW threshold for the open-access/IPP chapter. The 100 kW threshold is the 2025 position but the 2025 Regulations are currently STAYED by Kerala HC (10-Nov-2025).

values.raw_excerpt: 34.2 A consumer purchasing power from an IPP supplying power to a third party by availing open access shall pay to the licensee the following charges approved by the Commission from time to time: (i) Transmission charges; (ii) Wheeling charges; (iii) Cross subsidy surcharges; (iv) Transmission losses and Distribution losses
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 34.2); RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Reg 30)
values.caveat: CSS applies to third-party-sale (IPP) open-access RE consumers. CSS is expressly EXEMPT for captive consumers (Reg 33.2 proviso). The numeric CSS rate is set in KSERC tariff orders, not the RE regulations. The 2020 regs also establish CSS applicability.

values.raw_excerpt: The balance units, if any, will be carried forward to next billing period, after deducting a monthly banking charge of 2% of the balance units. This banking facility shall be allowed till the end of each financial year
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 28/29 banking provisions); RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Regs 20-21)
values.caveat: Banking is permitted for prosumers/captive consumers under both frameworks. The 2020 regs (banking with 5% charge per settlement period) remain the last unstayed framework, as the 2025 Regulations are STAYED by Kerala HC (10-Nov-2025).

values.raw_excerpt: The balance units, if any, will be carried forward to next billing period, after deducting a monthly banking charge of 2% of the balance units. This banking facility shall be allowed till the end of each financial year; ... The balance units remaining, if any, in the credit of the prosumer at the end of the financial year shall be monetized at the applicable APPC/ FiT
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 28(vi)-(viii))
values.caveat: Under the 2025 regs, banked energy carries forward (with 2% monthly deduction) until end of financial year, then unused credit is monetized at APPC/FiT (annual settlement). Under the 2020 regs, settlement period for non-solar (wind) sources is annual (1 April to 31 March). Both frameworks = annual settlement.

values.raw_excerpt: The balance units, if any, will be carried forward to next billing period, after deducting a monthly banking charge of 2% of the balance units.
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 28(vi))
values.caveat: CONFLICT BETWEEN REGIMES: 2025 regs = 2% monthly banking charge on balance units (Reg 28(vi)). Prior RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 = 5% of energy banked at end of billing period, non-cumulative (Reg 21(4)(i)). The 2025 regs (2% monthly) are STAYED by Kerala HC (10-Nov-2025); the 2020 figure (5% per settlement) is the last unstayed rate. Treat as contested until the HC matter resolves (next hearing 1-Dec-2025).

values.raw_excerpt: 'Banking facility' means such facility whereby the prosumer or the captive generator of Renewable Energy System injects ... and to be carried forward to the subsequent billing periods of the settlement period
values.source_name: KSERC RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Reg 2 definition + Regs 20-21); RE & Related Matters Regulations 2025 (Reg 28 banking)
values.caveat: Banking is defined for the prosumer/captive generator's own surplus carried forward against own consumption; the regulations do not provide a third-party (merchant/IPP-to-multiple-consumer) banking facility — banking is tied to self-consumption/net adjustment. Not fully explicit prohibition; confidence is estimated.

values.raw_excerpt: Financial Year ... HPO WPO DRE Other RPO Total RPO ESO | 2025 – 26  2.40  1.75  3.00  34.85  42.00  0
values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 41.1, Table 8)
values.caveat: Wind Power Obligation (WPO) FY2025-26 = 1.75%, rising to 1.80 (FY26-27), 1.90, 2.00, 2.20 (FY29-30). Wind RPO applies only to energy from wind projects commissioned after 31-Mar-2022 (per MoP order). The 2025 Regulations are STAYED by Kerala HC (10-Nov-2025); the 2024 amendment to the 2020 regs (which had identical FY2025-26 WPO=1.75) is the last unstayed RPO schedule.

values.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 41.1, Table 8)
values.caveat: Total RPO FY2025-26 = 42.00% (HPO 2.40 + WPO 1.75 + DRE 3.00 + Other RPO 34.85), rising to 50% by FY2029-30. CONFLICT: the 2024 Second Amendment (to 2020 regs) set FY2024-25 Total=40% and FY2025-26 Total=43%. The 2025 regs (Total=42%) are STAYED by Kerala HC; the 2024 amendment figures (40% FY24-25 / 43% FY25-26) are the last unstayed values. Reconcile after HC resolution.

values.raw_excerpt: All the renewable energy power plants, unless and otherwise exempted, shall be treated as 'MUST RUN' power plants and shall not be subjected to 'Merit order Dispatch' principles.
values.source_name: KSERC RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Reg 38(1) – Principles for dispatch)
values.caveat: Must-run status is express in the 2020 regs (Reg 38). The 2025 regs do not restate the phrase 'must run' but defer scheduling/dispatch to KSERC Forecasting, Scheduling, Deviation Settlement Regulations; RE must-run status is also mandated nationally (Electricity Act / IEGC 2023). Durable across both frameworks.

values.text: Wind power 'including repowering' is an eligible project category; ANERT (nodal agency) to revise the state wind atlas; detailed wind/repowering guidelines to follow. No quantified repowering incentive specified.
values.raw_excerpt: Eligible Clean Energy Projects: ... Wind Power (including repowering) ... 3.2.15 Detailed guidelines will follow the specific policies/guidelines prepared by the Government of Kerala for wind power development.
values.source_name: Draft Kerala Power Policy, 2025 (Dept. of Power, Govt. of Kerala) — Sections 3.2.x
values.caveat: SOURCE IS A DRAFT marked 'Draft for Discussion. NOT FOR EXTERNAL CIRCULATION' (dated Dec-2024). Repowering is named as an eligible category but no specific repowering incentive (tariff/CUF/depreciation benefit) is quantified — left to forthcoming guidelines. Not a notified policy; do not rely on for legal certainty until final Kerala Power Policy is gazetted.

values.text: Government/PSU land identified by ANERT under a revenue-sharing lease-rental model (land banks via District Level Committees); Clean Energy Projects treated as industrial (Land Reform Act ceiling relaxation), accorded Deemed Non-Agricultural status with land-conversion fee exempted; 100% stamp-duty reimbursement on private land purchase.
values.raw_excerpt: 3.5.6 ANERT will coordinate with various Government departments and state PSUs to identify available land ... under a revenue sharing based lease rental model ... 3.5.7 Clean Energy Projects may be treated as industrial projects for which relaxation of ceiling under Land Reform Act is already available. 3.5.8 Deemed Non-Agricultural status will be accorded ... Any applicable fee for such land conversion shall be exempted
values.source_name: Draft Kerala Power Policy, 2025 (Dept. of Power, Govt. of Kerala) — Sections 3.5.6-3.5.8 and stamp-duty provision
values.caveat: SOURCE IS A DRAFT ('Draft for Discussion. NOT FOR EXTERNAL CIRCULATION', Dec-2024) and not yet gazetted. Provisions cover land identification (lease-rental land banks), industrial/Land-Reform-ceiling relaxation, deemed-NA status with conversion-fee exemption, and 100% stamp-duty reimbursement on private land — but are not legally enforceable until the policy is notified.

values.raw_excerpt: 3.2.11 ANERT, under the Power Department, will act as the Nodal Agency, issuing technical approvals through a single window portal (K-SWIFT). Developers must obtain technical approval from ANERT, and all relevant departments and agencies will register in the portal.
values.source_name: Draft Kerala Power Policy, 2025 (Dept. of Power, Govt. of Kerala) — Section 3.2.11
values.caveat: Single-window clearance via ANERT/K-SWIFT is proposed in the DRAFT Kerala Power Policy 2025 ('Draft for Discussion. NOT FOR EXTERNAL CIRCULATION', Dec-2024). K-SWIFT is Kerala's existing investment single-window system, but its application to wind RE approvals is per this not-yet-notified draft; confirm against the gazetted final policy.

values.text: Not established for wind power generally. A 100% electricity-duty exemption for 10 years appears only for Green Hydrogen/Green Ammonia plants (Sec 4.1.10(4)); the draft cross-references exemptions under the separate Kerala Solar Policy and Kerala Small Hydro Policy.
values.raw_excerpt: 4.1.10 The incentives mentioned below shall apply to renewable electricity generation provided for producing green hydrogen/ammonia ... (4) 100 percent exemption from electricity duty for 10 years
values.source_name: Draft Kerala Power Policy, 2025 (Dept. of Power, Govt. of Kerala) — Section 4.1.10 (Green Hydrogen/Ammonia incentives)
values.caveat: The 100% electricity-duty exemption (and 50% wheeling / 100% CSS exemptions, 10 yrs) is scoped to GREEN HYDROGEN/AMMONIA plants, NOT general wind generation, and the source is a NON-NOTIFIED draft. The draft cross-refers wind/RE benefits to the (separate, not located here) Kerala Solar Policy and Kerala Small Hydro Policy. No primary, notified electricity-duty exemption specific to wind generation was found — effectively unresolved for wind.

values.raw_excerpt: Selection of projects under InSTS category (excluding open access projects) shall be through a competitive bidding process, as per the requirement of APDISCOMs to fulfil RPO target fixed by the APERC or any requirements beyond RPO target subject to the approval of the APERC. Project developer shall be allowed to develop the MW scale wind power projects through open access route under this Policy for sale of energy within the State subject to evacuation feasibility.
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37, Energy (Power-II) Dept, dt.30.10.2024), clause 7.1.1
values.caveat: AP has moved away from preferential/feed-in wind tariffs. New wind PPAs with DISCOMs are via competitive bidding (SECI/NTPC for ISTS; DISCOM bids for InSTS). No state-fixed preferential wind tariff exists under the 2024 policy. Policy valid 5 years from 30.10.2024.

values.raw_excerpt: The Clean Energy Projects not fulfilling the criteria of Captive generating plant shall be considered as third-party sale projects and Cross-subsidy surcharge and Additional surcharge shall be levied as determined by APERC from time to time for consumption from third party Clean Energy Projects.
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.8(a)
values.caveat: Third-party sale is permitted but attracts CSS and additional surcharge (no concession). Governed by APERC GEOA Regulation No. 3 of 2024.

values.raw_excerpt: All registered companies, Government entities, partnership companies/firms, individuals, and all consumers of APDISCOM(s) will be eligible for setting up of Clean Energy Projects within the State for sale of electricity/captive use, in accordance with the Electricity Act 2003.
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.2
values.caveat: Captive use is allowed; captive plants (per Electricity Rules) are exempt from CSS and additional surcharge and have no GEOA load limit (clauses 5.8, 5.10).

values.raw_excerpt: Open access shall be eligible for all consumers with contracted demand or sanctioned load of 100 kW or more through single connection or multiple connections aggregating to a 100 kW or more located in the same division of a DISCOM, except for captive consumers who shall not have any load limitation.
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.10(b); confirmed in APERC GEOA Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 2.1(f)
values.caveat: 100 kW threshold for green energy open access; no load limit for captive consumers. Per APERC GEOA Regulation No. 3 of 2024 (effective 01.05.2024).

values.raw_excerpt: Wheeling Charges for 5th Control Period. Voltage / Financial Year 2024-25 ... 33 kV (Rs./KWH) 0.32 ... 11 kV (Rs./KWH) 0.62 ... LT (Rs./KWH) 1.09
values.source_name: APERC Multi-Year Order on Determination of Wheeling Tariffs of DISCOMs' network for 5th Control Period FY2024-25 to FY2028-29 (Press Note dt.27.09.2024)
values.caveat: Wheeling charge at 33 kV (primary transmission voltage). Voltage-level-specific charges: 11 kV ₹0.62/kWh, LT ₹1.09/kWh (FY2024-25). Rises each year; FY2025-26: 33kV ₹0.38/kWh. Waived if injection and withdrawal at same voltage level (policy clause 5.7). Order covers FY2024-25 to FY2028-29.

values.raw_excerpt: These charges, which are now uniform and similar to the retail supply tariffs, come with a fifty per cent concession for domestic consumers, public utilities, and government institutions.
values.source_name: APERC Multi-Year Wheeling Tariff Order, 5th Control Period (Press Note dt.27.09.2024), Important Directions (i)
values.caveat: The 50% concession applies only to domestic/public-utility/government consumers, NOT to commercial/industrial GEOA users. GEOA RE consumers otherwise pay full wheeling charges (GEOA Reg 3/2024 clause 12 grants no concession).

values.raw_excerpt: As per Section 42 (2) of the Electricity Act, 2003, open access shall be allowed on payment of a surcharge in addition to the charges for wheeling as may be determined by the State Commission. Hence, the Commission is not inclined to grant any exemption or concession in this regard... Cross subsidy Surcharge as applicable, and determined by the Commission in Retail Supply Tariff Order... shall be paid by the GEOA consumers.
values.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 12(c) and clause 13
values.caveat: CSS IS applicable to GEOA/third-party consumers. NOT leviable for captive consumers, waste-to-energy supply, or green hydrogen/ammonia production. CSS value itself is set in the periodic Retail Supply Tariff Order, not fixed in this regulation.

values.raw_excerpt: The CSS and AS are determined following the provisions in the Electricity Act, 2003 and the NTP. The Commission is not inclined to grant any exemption or concession to GEOA on these aspects so as to balance the interests of the consumers at large and hence the draft is retained.
values.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 13 (Commission's Analysis and Decision)
values.caveat: Stakeholders requested 50% CSS / waiver; APERC explicitly declined. Full CSS applies (0% concession) except full exemption for captive, WtE, and green hydrogen/ammonia. RE Manufacturing projects get 10-year CSS exemption (policy clause 5.8(b)).

values.raw_excerpt: The Cross Subsidy Surcharge, and Additional Surcharge shall be determined as per the provisions of the Electricity Act, 2003 and the National Tariff Policy... Provided that cross subsidy surcharge and additional surcharge shall not be leviable in case open access is provided to a person who has established a captive generating plant... Provided also that an additional surcharge shall not be applicable in case electricity is produced from offshore wind projects, which are commissioned up to December 2032.
values.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 13
values.caveat: Additional surcharge is applicable to third-party GEOA. Exempt for captive, WtE, green hydrogen/ammonia, and offshore wind commissioned up to Dec 2032. Quantum set per Electricity Act/NTP via tariff orders.

values.raw_excerpt: APPROVED: TRANSMISSION LOSS TRAJECTORY AND SYSTEM AVAILABILITY ... 2024-25 99.70% 2.75 2.95% 2.55% ... 2025-26 99.70% 2.75 ... 2026-27 99.70% 2.70
values.source_name: APERC Transmission Tariff Order for 5th Control Period FY2024-25 to FY2028-29, Table No. 3.2
values.caveat: 2.75% is the approved AVERAGE intra-state transmission loss for FY2024-25 and FY2025-26 (band 2.55%-2.95%); declines to 2.70% from FY2026-27. This is the InSTS transmission loss only; DISTRIBUTION/WHEELING losses are separate, voltage-pair and DISCOM specific (e.g. APSPDCL LT-to-LT ~4.13%, 33kV-to-33kV ~2.99% in FY2024-25 per the Wheeling Tariff Order).

values.raw_excerpt: Banking: No generator other than the Wind, Solar and Mini Hydel power generators shall be allowed the facility of banking which shall be subject to the following conditions.
values.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 14
values.caveat: Banking allowed specifically for Wind, Solar and Mini-Hydel generators. Effective 01.05.2024; subject to first amendment (Regulation 3 of 2024 amendment process ongoing 2025).

values.raw_excerpt: The Banking shall be on a monthly billing cycle basis. Each calendar month shall be treated as one billing cycle. The banked energy shall be utilized within the same billing cycle failing which the unutilized energy at the end of the billing cycle shall lapse, and no compensation whatsoever shall be claimed/paid for such lapsed banked energy.
values.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 14(1)
values.caveat: Monthly billing-cycle banking; unutilised energy lapses at month-end with no compensation. Permitted banked quantum capped at 30% of consumer's total monthly DISCOM consumption.

values.raw_excerpt: The Banking charges in kind shall be 8% of the energy banked at the consumer end.
values.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 14(2)
values.caveat: Banking charge is 8% in kind of energy banked. The 12% peak-ToD charge that appeared in the DRAFT clause 14(4) was removed from the final adopted regulation. Banked quantum limited to 30% of monthly DISCOM consumption. Drawal not permitted in peak hours during R&C measures.

values.raw_excerpt: The permitted quantum of banked energy by the Green Energy Open Access consumers shall not be more than thirty percent of the total monthly consumption of electricity from the distribution licensee by the consumers.
values.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 14
values.caveat: Banking is available to GEOA consumers generally (both captive and third-party open-access wind/solar/mini-hydel), not restricted to captive. The regulation applies banking conditions to 'Green Energy Open Access consumers' without excluding third-party transactions.

values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), Section 3 (MoP GoI order dt.22.07.2022 trajectory table)

APERC's own Regulation No. 5 of 2022 specifies a single combined Total RPO (20-24%), not a separate binding wind sub-obligation. Use with care: the wind figure is the central trajectory, not an APERC-adopted wind-only obligation.

values.raw_excerpt: Must run Status: Injection from Clean Energy Projects covered under the policy scope shall be considered to be deemed scheduled.
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.17
values.caveat: Wind (a Clean Energy Project under policy scope) injection is deemed scheduled (must-run treatment), consistent with central must-run status for RE.

values.text: Repowering promoted for WTGs operating >=10 years per National Repowering & Life Extension Policy 2023; original PPA continues for last-3-year average generation, additional generation sold via competitive bidding or for captive/group-captive/third-party sale within/outside State
values.raw_excerpt: The Government of Andhra Pradesh will promote repowering of existing wind turbines which have completed at least 10 years in operation... In the case of power being procured by AP discoms through PPA, the power generated corresponding to average of last three years' generation prior to repowering would continue to be procured on the terms of PPA in-force... and remaining additional generation may be purchased by discoms... at a tariff discovered through competitive bidding... The repowered wind projects shall also be allowed to use the additional power for captive/group captive or third-party sale within and/or outside the State.
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 7.1.3
values.caveat: No direct financial subsidy for repowering; the incentive is facilitative (PPA continuity for baseline generation + flexibility to sell additional generation). Aligned to National Repowering & Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects, 2023.

values.text: Government/revenue land allotted on alienation basis, lease up to 30 years (33 yrs for PSP/hydro), at INR 31,000/acre/year with 5% escalation every 2 years; per AP Land Allotment Policy 2012
values.raw_excerpt: Government/ Revenue/concerned department to allot the land to the Developer on alienation basis for lease up to a maximum 30 years... The land allotment shall be done as per the existing Andhra Pradesh Land Allotment Policy 2012... The land lease rate for revenue/government/private/patta land will be INR 31,000/acre/year with 5% escalation for every 2 years for Clean Energy Projects (except for biofuels & GH Hub).
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.3
values.caveat: Wind net-worth eligibility for resource allocation is INR 100 Lakhs/MW (clause 5.2). Lease rate INR 31,000/acre/yr (+5% per 2 yrs). Deemed Non-Agricultural status granted with land-conversion fee exemption (clause 5.13).

values.text: No general exemption for wind generation projects (electricity duty payable as determined by GoAP); 10-year reimbursement only for RE Manufacturing projects
values.raw_excerpt: Electricity Duty for Clean Energy Projects shall be paid as determined by the Government of Andhra Pradesh and its amendments from time to time. In case of RE Manufacturing Projects, the electricity duty shall be reimbursed for a period of 10 years irrespective of Discom/ Open Access/ captive/third party procurement.
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.9
values.caveat: The 2024 ICE Policy does NOT grant a blanket electricity-duty exemption for wind generation (unlike the earlier 2018 Wind Policy which had exemptions). Only RE Manufacturing projects (e.g. wind turbine manufacturing) get a 10-year electricity-duty reimbursement (clause 7.2.2(g)).

values.raw_excerpt: Single Window Clearance: NREDCAP will develop a portal for facilitating single window clearance for all projects under this policy. The services of this single window clearance portal will be made available to all the projects under this policy for obtaining time bound statutory clearances.
values.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.16
values.caveat: Single-window clearance provided via NREDCAP (State Nodal Agency) portal. Projects also exempt from AP Pollution Control Board NOC/Consent for establishment (clause 5.14).

Bifurcated by capacity: wind <10 MW = project_specific (Sec 62); wind >=10 MW = competitive_bidding (Sec 63). Captured value reflects the project_specific regime applicable to small/below-threshold wind; large wind in MP is in practice competitive_bidding.
values.source_name: MPERC (Tariff Determination of Energy from Renewable Energy Sources) (Revision-I) Regulations, 2024 {G-43(I) of 2024}
values.raw_excerpt: 4.1 ... a) Wind Power Project - using new Wind Turbine generators with capacity less than 10 MW. ... 4.2 The Tariff determined for the following categories of Projects for which Central Government has already issued guidelines for Tariff Based Competitive Bidding shall be adopted by the Commission under Section 63 ... a) Wind Power Projects using new Wind Turbine generators with capacity equal to or more than 10 MW. 4.3 The Commission shall determine Tariff for RE Projects below threshold limit of eligibility for participating in Competitive Bidding on case to case basis as per the Project Specific Tariff determination process provided in Regulation 7.

values.caveat: Third-party sale of RE/wind power is permitted via intra-state open access; third-party consumers must pay all open access charges (wheeling, CSS, additional surcharge) and bear losses.
values.source_name: MPERC (Cogeneration and Generation of Electricity from Renewable Sources of Energy) Regulations (as amended) – summary; and MPERC (Terms and Conditions for Intra-State Open Access in Madhya Pradesh) Regulations, 2021 (Revision-I)
values.raw_excerpt: The Captive RE Generation Plant shall also be eligible to sale its surplus power to any 3rd party subject to provisions of these Regulations provided that: The expenses, if any, incurred on the infrastructure development for evacuation and supply of power are required to be borne by the owner of Captive RE Generation Plant or the 3rd Party consumer as the case may be.

values.caveat: Captive (incl. group captive) RE generation is permitted. Captive RE consumers are exempt from CSS but must pay wheeling + additional surcharge and bear losses. Note: DISCOM-registered captive RE projects are NOT eligible for banking; non-registered captive projects retain banking eligibility.
values.source_name: MPERC (Cogeneration and Generation of Electricity from Renewable Sources of Energy) Regulations – captive RE provisions (per Mercom report of MPERC amendment)
values.raw_excerpt: the captive consumer will not be liable to pay cross subsidy surcharge, wheeling, and additional surcharges. That said, they will be liable to bear the losses for carrying the generated electricity from its project to the destination.

values.caveat: 100 kW is the Green Energy Open Access threshold per the MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 (Rule 4) which MPERC adopted via its 2023 GEOA Methodology Regulations. Conventional (non-green) intra-state OA threshold remains 1 MW.
values.source_name: MPERC (Methodology for determination of Open Access charges and Banking charges for Green Energy Open Access consumers) Regulations, 2023 [No. MPERC-2023-497], aligned with MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules, 2022
values.raw_excerpt: These Regulations shall be called Madhya Pradesh Electricity Regulatory Commission (Methodology for determination of Open Access charges and Banking charges for Green Energy Open Access consumers) Regulations 2023 ... [Green Open Access limit reduced from 1 MW to 100 kW for green energy].

values.caveat: Value is the 33 kV wheeling charge (typical wind interconnection voltage) for FY 2026-27 (current). The recon file cited 0.19 INR/kWh for FY2025-26, but that is now superseded by the FY2026-27 order (PNo 140/2025). Before any wind-policy 50% wheeling waiver (RE Policy 2025) is applied.
values.source_name: MPERC Aggregate Revenue Requirement & Retail Supply Tariff Order for FY 2026-27 (Petition No. 140/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: at 33 kV the wheeling charge is 0.17 Rs/kWh as per the Commission-approved Table 89 in the FY2026-27 order.

values.caveat: 50% waiver on MPERC-specified wheeling charges for 5 years from COD for RE projects registered under the RE Policy 2025 (carried over from RE Policy 2022). Subject to MPERC approval 'from time to time'.
values.source_name: Madhya Pradesh Renewable Energy Policy 2025 (NRED, Notification No. 108, dated 04/04/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: Waiver of wheeling charges: Facility of wheeling will be available to all RE power projects through MPPTCL/ MP Discoms, as case may be, as per wheeling charges specified by MPERC. 50% waiver on wheeling charges shall be applicable or as may be approved by Madhya Pradesh Electricity Regulatory Commission from time to time. This waiver shall be applicable for 5 years from COD.

values.caveat: CSS applies to third-party open-access sale of wind power (category-wise, per the formula in the retail tariff order). CSS is EXEMPT for captive RE consumers and is WAIVED for Green Zone projects under RE Policy 2025. Revised annually.
values.source_name: MPERC Aggregate Revenue Requirement & Retail Supply Tariff Order for FY 2025-26 (Petition No. 72/2024, dated 29/03/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: Wheeling charges, Cross subsidy surcharge and additional surcharge on the Wheeling charges and such other charges shall be applicable at the rate decided by the commission from time to time and its retail supply tariff order.

values.caveat: Additional surcharge is levied on open-access consumers (incl. wind OA and captive); Commission-determined value for FY 2025-26 = Rs 1.24/kWh, for FY 2026-27 = Rs 1.18/kWh. Revised annually in the retail tariff order.
values.source_name: MPERC Aggregate Revenue Requirement & Retail Supply Tariff Order for FY 2025-26 (Petition No. 72/2024, dated 29/03/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: Table 97: Determination of Additional Surcharge for FY 2025-26 — Admitted Fixed Cost of Thermal Generating Sources for FY 2025-26 = Rs 11,048.93 Crore ... C=A/B*10 = Rs 1.24 /kWh. ... The Petitioners have thus claimed the Additional Surcharge of Rs. 1.44 per unit on the power drawn by the Open Access consumers.

values.caveat: 2.60% is the intra-state (MPPTCL) transmission loss for FY 2026-27 (current). The recon file cited 2.61% for FY2025-26, which is now superseded. Distribution/wheeling losses are higher and voltage-dependent (EHT 3.62%, 33 kV 4.18%, 11 kV 5.23%, LT 6.06%). Revised annually.
values.source_name: MPERC Aggregate Revenue Requirement & Retail Supply Tariff Order for FY 2026-27 (Petition No. 140/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: The Intra-State transmission losses of 2.60% for FY 2026-27 based on FY2024-25 actuals.

values.caveat: Banking is allowed for Green Energy OA consumers under the 2023 Methodology Regulations. Exception: DISCOM-registered captive RE projects are NOT eligible for banking.
values.source_name: MPERC (Methodology for determination of Open Access charges and Banking charges for Green Energy Open Access consumers) Regulations, 2023 (as amended)
values.raw_excerpt: Banking Charges in Madhya Pradesh are adjusted at 8% of the energy banked, and banking of energy is permitted only on a monthly basis as per calendar month. ... the credit for banked energy banked during a month shall not be permitted to be carried forward to subsequent months and the credit shall be adjusted during the same month.

values.caveat: Current Green Energy OA banking cycle is MONTHLY (calendar month, no carry-forward) under the 2023 Methodology Regulations. The older Cogeneration Regulations provided ANNUAL (financial-year) banking.
values.source_name: MPERC (Methodology for determination of Open Access charges and Banking charges for Green Energy Open Access consumers) Regulations, 2023 (as amended)
values.raw_excerpt: banking of energy is permitted only on a monthly basis as per calendar month ... the credit for banked energy banked during a month shall not be permitted to be carried forward to subsequent months and the credit shall be adjusted during the same month.

values.caveat: Banking charge = 8% of energy banked (adjusted in kind). Aligns with the Forum of Regulators / MoP model GEOA regulation.
values.source_name: MPERC (Methodology for determination of Open Access charges and Banking charges for Green Energy Open Access consumers) Regulations, 2023 (as amended)
values.raw_excerpt: Banking Charges in Madhya Pradesh are adjusted at 8% of the energy banked

values.caveat: Banking is framed for captive consumption. GEOA Methodology bars banking for 'RE plants supplying power to third party under open access'. DISCOM-registered captive projects are excluded from banking entirely.
values.source_name: MPERC (Methodology for determination of Open Access charges and Banking charges for Green Energy Open Access consumers) Regulations, 2023
values.raw_excerpt: The drawl of banked energy by Green Energy Open Access Consumer shall be subject to scheduling without any capping, and unadjusted banked energy shall lapse on completion of banking cycle.

values.caveat: MoP's revised category-wise RPO/RCO notification (Oct 2023, applicable FY2024-25 onward) sets FY2025-26 Wind = 1.45%. This is the current MoP-aligned figure; an earlier MP-notified 3.36% (from July-2022 MoP trajectory) is superseded at MoP level.
values.source_name: MPERC (Cogeneration and Generation of Electricity from Renewable Sources of Energy) (Revision-II) Regulations, 2021 – Fifth Amendment (RPO trajectory aligned to MoP RCO Notification); Prayas (Energy Group) comments
values.raw_excerpt: 2025-26 Wind 1.45% Hydro 1.22% Distributed 2.10% Other 28.24% Total 33.01% ; 2026-27 Wind 1.97% ... Total 35.95% (MoP-aligned trajectory adopted by MPERC 5th Amendment).

values.caveat: MoP total RPO/RCO for FY2025-26 = 33.01% confirmed by multiple independent sources. An earlier MP-notified 30.97% (from July-2022 MoP trajectory) is superseded.
values.source_name: MPERC (Cogeneration and Generation of Electricity from Renewable Sources of Energy) (Revision-II) Regulations, 2021 – Fifth Amendment; Prayas (Energy Group) comments

values.caveat: RE 'must-run' status derives from the central Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy through GEOA) Rules / Tariff Policy 2016 and is operationalised in MP via the Cogeneration Revision-II Regs 2021 + MP Grid Code 2024.
values.source_name: MPERC (Tariff Determination of Energy from Renewable Energy Sources) (Revision-I) Regulations, 2024 {G-43(I) of 2024}
values.raw_excerpt: 12.4 The applicability of Merit Order Despatch for RE Power Projects shall be governed as per the provisions under MPERC (Cogeneration and Generation of Electricity from Renewable Sources of Energy) (Revision II) Regulations 2021 and subsequent amendments thereof.

values.text: Repowering facilitated: continued PPA at pre-agreed rate for energy equal to avg of last 3 years' generation (PPA period extendable to offset transition loss); land-use facilitation; LUPA extended for revised useful life at 100% of DLC rates; MPPTCL evacuation/substation upgrade at its rates; NRED to issue detailed repowering guidelines.
values.caveat: RE Policy 2025 explicitly targets wind repowering with PPA continuity, land-use facilitation and evacuation support; no fixed monetary incentive/tariff adder is specified.
values.source_name: Madhya Pradesh Renewable Energy Policy 2025 (NRED, Notification No. 108, dated 04/04/2025) – Wind Repowering provisions
values.raw_excerpt: The policy also allows Madhya Pradesh Power Management Company Ltd. (MPPMCL) and distribution licensees to continue procuring power equivalent to the average generation over the last three years before the repowering request, with the possibility of extending the PPA period to compensate for the loss of generation during the transition. ... Existing land use permissions under the Land Use Permission Agreement (LUPA) may be extended for the revised useful life of the project, with developers required to pay 100 per cent of the District Level Committee (DLC) rates for the remaining period.

values.text: Government land (if available) on concessional rate = 50% rebate on circle rate; 50% reimbursement of stamp duty on purchase of private land; land allotment/transfer/surrender governed via online portal + District Level Implementation Committees.
values.caveat: Government land at 50% circle-rate rebate + 50% stamp-duty reimbursement on private land. Carried over from RE Policy 2022.
values.source_name: Madhya Pradesh Renewable Energy Policy 2025 (NRED, Notification No. 108, dated 04/04/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: Government Land on concessional rate: Government land, if available, shall be provided on concessional rate (rebate of 50% on circle rate) to developers. ... 50% reimbursement on stamp duty on purchase of private land for the project shall be available to developers.

values.text: 100% exemption for 10 years from COD
values.caveat: 100% electricity duty exemption on generation for 10 years from COD, under the MP Vidyut Shulk Adhiniyam 2012.
values.source_name: Madhya Pradesh Renewable Energy Policy 2025 (NRED, Notification No. 108, dated 04/04/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: Exemption in Electricity Duty and Energy Development Cess: As per the provisions of Madhya Pradesh Vidyut Shulk Adhiniyam, 2012, projects shall be entitled to receive hundred percent (100%) exemption from payment of Electricity Duty on generation of electrical energy for a period of ten (10) years from the date of COD.

values.caveat: MP levies an 'Energy Development Cess' (not a 'green energy cess'); RE projects pay NO such cess for 10 years from COD. There is no per-kWh green cess imposed on RE.
values.source_name: Madhya Pradesh Renewable Energy Policy 2025 (NRED, Notification No. 108, dated 04/04/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: No energy development cess shall be payable on the power supplied by renewable energy projects for a period of ten (10) years from the COD.

values.caveat: RE Policy 2025 establishes a Single Window System plus District Level Implementation Committees and an online registration portal.
values.source_name: Madhya Pradesh Renewable Energy Policy 2025 (NRED, Notification No. 108, dated 04/04/2025)
values.raw_excerpt: District Level Implementation Committees (DLICs) and a Single Window System are established to streamline processes and provide support to developers.
